<title>ACKNOWLEDGMENTS </title>

I would like to thank my major professor, Dr. Mark Mba-Wright for his guidance, knowledge, support, and patience throughout my degree. He has inspired me to view the world with a curious mind. I would also like to thank my family and friends for their support and love during this time.
<title>ABSTRACT </title>

This thesis describes an analysis of the economic and environmental implications of anaerobic digestion (AD) process additions for upgrading biogas to renewable natural gas (RNG). These integrations are designed to increase the efficiency of feedstocks used for AD. The addition of a pretreatment solution and various co-digestion ratios are analyzed to increase feedstock digestibility. Two papers are included: "Techno-economic and environmental assessment of converting mixed prairie to renewable natural gas with co-product hydroxycinnamic acid" and "Integration of biogas model (ADM1) for economic and environmental analysis of RNG production".

Results from the pretreatment addition of the first paper show that increasing the efficiency of the feedstock decreases the minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) due to higher methane (CH4) yields. The MFSP of the scenarios involving pretreatment decreased by 12% to 34% due to the biomass digestibility increasing and collection of hydroxycinnamic acid (HCA), a co-product from the pretreatment process. These system updates led to environmental impact changes due to the integration of pretreatment chemicals. All analyzed scenarios producing RNG emit less greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions when compared to the production of fossil fuel natural gas.

Results from the second paper, involving the co-digestion of biomass and manure with no pretreatment, showed the addition of manure decreased the MFSP by 7% to 19% when added to prairie biomass. While our biogas model did not fully capture the co-digestion relationship seen within experimental literature for these feedstocks, two scenarios of co-digestion had a lower MFSP than the mono-digestion of cattle manure. Additionally, the feedstock source greatly impacts the environmental results. Impact categories analyzed include the potential for global

warming, acidification, ecotoxicity, eutrophication, ozone depletion, photochemical oxidation, carcinogenics, non-carcinogenics, and respiratory effects. Avoidance of manure alleviates burdens while the cultivation of prairie biomass can require additional resources and therefore impact system burdens. Prairie biomass cultivation alleviated burdens within the ecotoxicity category due to beneficial impacts on the environment.

Farm AD is a viable method for alternative energy sources and additional revenue streams. Prairie biomass was determined to be an ideal candidate for co-digestion due to the positive impacts seen from cultivation. This paper exemplifies benefits of prairie biomass use for co-digestion, including synergistic properties and additional co-products from prairie biomass pretreatment.

1: <header>Introduction </header>

The importance of carbon reduction within the U.S. is apparent with the re-entry into The Paris Climate Agreement in 2021 and the availability of tradable credits awarded to economize the reduction of emissions (Blinken, 2021; U.S. EPA, 2023a). The proposed plan to meet carbon neutrality within the U.S. includes a reduction in fossil fuel consumption, production, and investment in low-carbon technologies (Chen et al., 2022). Various technologies independent of fossil fuels have been explored, including wind, solar photovoltaics, and nuclear energy (Handayani et al., 2019). Additionally, there are various government grants gifted for the implementation of greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction strategies, such as the $5 billion Climate Pollution Reduction Grant (U.S. EPA, 2024a). In 2023, the White House held the first Methane Summit where plans for methane (CH4) detection and reduction were discussed, highlighting the need to decrease CH4 emitted from the oil and gas sector (The White House, 2023).

In 2021, the agricultural sector emitted 10% of the total GHG emissions within the U.S. (U.S. EPA, 2024b). In 2023, beef production alone accounted for 3.7% of the total GHG emissions as one cow can produce between 70 to 120 kg of CH4 every year (Rotz, 2023; U.S. EPA, 2020). Effective employment of organic materials, such as manure, for energy could alleviate problems surrounding the waste management crisis and allow farmers to diversify their products while providing energy from alternative sources (Harshwardhan & Upadhyay et al., 2017; Wainaina et al., 2020). Organic residues such as livestock manure can be converted to clean energy by biochemical or thermochemical means, such as incineration or composting (Gutiérrez et al., 2020; Siwal et al., 2021). In addition to upgrading residues for energy, many researchers have discussed the importance of utilizing biomass for energy, due to its regenerative

capabilities (Ahorsu et al., 2018). Using biomass to produce energy and chemicals is a viable method to meet consumer demands in a sustainable manner (Kumar & Verma, 2021). Exploiting the carbon sequestered from photosynthesis creates additional carbon sinks within the carbon cycle and may reduce the use of material resources (Tan & Lamers, 2021). Specially, cultivation of various energy dense biomass species can contribute to sustainable systems while providing beneficial ecological impacts, such as prairie biomass (McLaughlin et al., 2002). Conversion of manure and biomass on farms is becoming increasingly popular with anaerobic digestion (AD) (USDA, 2023).

AD is the biological transformation of organic matter to biogas, which typically has a CH4 content of 40% to 75%, depending on the feedstock used (Bharathiraja et al., 2018). AD is revered for its independence from foreign fuels, GHGs reduction capabilities, and odor reduction potential (Chowdhury, 2021; Pucker et al., 2013). Over ten million metric tons of carbon dioxide (CO2) equivalent GHGs were estimated to be reduced from the digestion of livestock manure in 2022 (U.S. EPA, 2023b). Upgrading biogas to a CH4-rich stream known as renewable natural gas (RNG) has several uses, where it has been determined to be especially impactful for GHG reduction of heavy-duty transportation (Budzianowski & Brodacka, 2017; Shanmugam et al., 2018).

The goal of this thesis is to provide insight into the impacts and feasibility of upgrading biogas from AD, specifically with an economic and environmental focus. Efficiency of feedstock utilization is examined with biomass pretreatment and the use of multiple feedstocks for digestion. Various scenarios were examined to determine the environmental impacts and economic implications of an RNG biorefinery.

0: <title>Background </title>

AD is the biological breakdown of organic matter without oxygen present. The product of AD is biogas, largely composed of CH4 and CO2, with trace gases such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (Ward et al., 2008). The biogas stream can be combusted on-site or upgraded further. RNG, the upgraded biogas stream made up almost entirely of CH4, offers the same product as natural gas from sustainable sources such as food waste or biomass. The residue leftover from AD, known as digestate, is dense with nutrients like N and phosphorus (P) and is often applied to crop fields (García-López et al., 2023). The four steps of AD include hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis and methanogenesis, with each step relying on different microbial groups that require unique optimal environmental conditions (Meegoda et al., 2018). Other parameters affecting digestion include the hydraulic retention time, pH, loading rate, and the temperature of the digester (Meegoda et al., 2018). Typical feedstocks of AD include municipal solids waste, food waste, livestock manure, and agricultural residues due to their availability.

Several parts of the world utilize AD, with Germany leading expansion of digestion with over 10,000 projects reported in 2018 (Cai et al., 2021). Within the U.S., there were 343 on-farm AD operations from livestock manure in 2023 (U.S. EPA, 2023b). However, without proper system optimization, AD systems can produce low CH4 yields or have common problems with system wide instability (Wu et al., 2021a). The primary reason cited for biogas operation failure is insufficient profit from low biogas yields (Nguyen et al., 2015). One case study estimated a farm selling electricity from AD lost $10,000 during a two-week shutdown from a system failure (Labatut & Gooch, 2012). Boosting system efficiency optimizes the produced CH4 yields, ultimately leading to an increase in savings. Possible efficiency strategies include increasing
feedstock's bioavailability during digestion or utilizing recycle loops downstream (Wu et al., 2021b).

While materials like manure are inexpensive and readily available, they can often lack the nutrients required for larger gas production (Bardi et al., 2023). Specifically, the carbon to nitrogen ratio (C/N) is often too low within livestock manure, causing issues within the system due to the low amount of energy present (Neshat et al., 2017). Conversely, the C/N ratio of lignocellulosic material, a very complex substrate, is often too high for proper digestion (Neshat et al., 2017). Digesting various feedstocks at the same, known as co-digestion, can provide better nutrients to the microbes and decrease toxicity (Siddique & Wahid, 2018). Co-digestion is typically recommended for efficiency and stability purposes (Bhatt & Tao, 2020). However, this feedstock integration can lead to an increase in price if more costly feedstocks are used, like energy crops. Velásquez Piñas et al. (2019) determined the mono-digestion of cattle manure to be more cost effective for smaller sized plants, while co-digestion was beneficial for larger scale digestion systems, where over 1000 kWe is produced (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

Another approach to increase bioavailability of feedstocks is to include a pretreatment process. This is a common approach for lignocellulosic biomass integration into biorefineries as the material is often difficult to digest (Kumar et al., 2020). Lignocellulosic biomass consists of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, making up 35-50%, 20-35%, and 5-30% of the material respectively (Kassaye, et al., 2016). The intricate network of hydroxyl groups within the cellulose and the rigid bindings of the three fractions creates a complex substrate for digestion (Mankar et al., 2021). Pretreatment of lignocellulosic material can increase bioavailability by reducing the recalcitrance and opening the structure further, therefore increasing accessibility (Bhutto et al., 2017; Fernandes et al., 2009). A number of pretreatment options for lignin

degradation have been researched such as steam, dilute-acid, or enzymatic pretreatments methods (Galbe & Zacchi, 2012). Matching co-digestion, the addition of pretreatment chemicals, higher system energy requirements, or upstream units can add to environmental impacts and operational costs of the plant (Baruah et al., 2018; Rezania et al., 2020).

To increase the usability of biogas, technologies for upgrading the produced biogas are implemented into the system (Kapoor et al., 2019). H2S and CO2 are usually targeted for removal within upgrading facilities. These removal steps are found to typically remove other trace elements found within the gas stream such as water vapor, ammonia, or siloxane (Salihu & Alam, 2015). The concentrations of these gas contaminants are dependent on the feedstock composition and can become corrosive during combustion if not removed beforehand (Awe et al., 2017; Khan et al., 2021). H2S is toxic and corrosive, typically found in levels of 100 ppm to 10,000 ppm within biogas and can negatively affect downstream units such as boilers and CO2 towers (Atelge et al., 2021, Abatzoglou & Boivin, 2008).

Depending on biogas use, the gas stream can be partitioned for combustion or for CO2 removal after H2S removal. Even without upgrading, biogas combusted on-site for electricity still has environmental improvements when compared to conventional electricity, reducing the resources used or CO2 equivalence emitted when compared to conventional electricity (de Meester, et al., 2012; Kaparaju & Rintala, 2011). There are several different methods that can be used for CO2 removal including membranes, pressure swing adsorption, and water scrubbers (Petersson & Wellinger, 2009). While gas upgrading does increase energy density and therefore optimizes the potential for uses, gas upgrading has large capital costs that act as a barrier for further implementation (Vasco-Correa et al., 2018).

Vo et al. (2018) completed an economic analysis on various CO2 separation technologies from the digestion of grass silage and dairy manure, determining amine scrubbing to be more cost effective than biological methanation. With CO2 removed, the remaining biomethane can be used for several applications including energy for fuel cells, transportational use, or industrial purposes (Andriani et al., 2014). Injection of RNG into a natural gas pipeline is a major capital cost, requiring even more capital for upgrading (Peppers et al., 2019). Other factors influencing the environmental impact of an AD system include biomass cultivation, feedstock transportation, and CH4 losses (Bacenetti et al., 2013).

Various analytical perspectives are required for a fuller understanding of complex systems, such as a biogas upgrading facility. Economic and environmental analyses can estimate indicators that can be further used for comparison or optimization, such as the cost of a product or the emissions from the system. A techno-economic analysis (TEA) is an analysis tool that evaluates the feasibility of a certain system, breaking down the process for profit optimization (Giacomella, 2021; Mahmud et al., 2021). The minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) is a common estimated parameter from a TEA that can be used for further comparison within clean energy systems (Batan et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2016; Lan et al., 2021; Mahmud et al., 2021). Additionally, a life cycle assessment (LCA) works to quantify the effect a product or process has on the surrounding environment (Góralczyk, 2003). Typical indicators for an LCA include estimation of the global warming potential (GWP) or eutrophication of a system (Abusoglu et al., 2017; Goglio et al., 2012; Xue et al., 2016). The combination of TEA and LCA provides meaningful insights for a system, clarifying areas that may need to be targeted for improvement to assist in further adoption of a technology (Wunderlich et al., 2021).
1: <title>Background </title>

The product of AD is biogas, largely composed of CH4 and CO2, with trace gases such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (Ward et al., 2008). The biogas stream can be combusted on-site or upgraded further. RNG, the upgraded biogas stream made up almost entirely of CH4, offers the same product as natural gas from sustainable sources such as food waste or biomass. Other parameters affecting digestion include the hydraulic retention time, pH, loading rate, and the temperature of the digester (Meegoda et al., 2018). Matching co-digestion, the addition of pretreatment chemicals, higher system energy requirements, or upstream units can add to environmental impacts and operational costs of the plant (Baruah et al., 2018; Rezania et al., 2020).

To increase the usability of biogas, technologies for upgrading the produced biogas are implemented into the system (Kapoor et al., 2019). H2S and CO2 are usually targeted for removal within upgrading facilities. These removal steps are found to typically remove other trace elements found within the gas stream such as water vapor, ammonia, or siloxane (Salihu & Alam, 2015). The concentrations of these gas contaminants are dependent on the feedstock composition and can become corrosive during combustion if not removed beforehand (Awe et al., 2017; Khan et al., 2021). H2S is toxic and corrosive, typically found in levels of 100 ppm to 10,000 ppm within biogas and can negatively affect downstream units such as boilers and CO2 towers (Atelge et al., 2021, Abatzoglou & Boivin, 2008).

Depending on biogas use, the gas stream can be partitioned for combustion or for CO2 removal after H2S removal. Even without upgrading, biogas combusted on-site for electricity still has environmental improvements when compared to conventional electricity, reducing the resources used or CO2 equivalence emitted when compared to conventional electricity (de Meester, et al., 2012; Kaparaju & Rintala, 2011). There are several different methods that can be used for CO2 removal including membranes, pressure swing adsorption, and water scrubbers (Petersson & Wellinger, 2009). While gas upgrading does increase energy density and therefore optimizes the potential for uses, gas upgrading has large capital costs that act as a barrier for further implementation (Vasco-Correa et al., 2018).

Vo et al. (2018) completed an economic analysis on various CO2 separation technologies from the digestion of grass silage and dairy manure, determining amine scrubbing to be more cost effective than biological methanation. With CO2 removed, the remaining biomethane can be used for several applications including energy for fuel cells, transportational use, or industrial purposes (Andriani et al., 2014). Injection of RNG into a natural gas pipeline is a major capital cost, requiring even more capital for upgrading (Peppers et al., 2019). Other factors influencing the environmental impact of an AD system include biomass cultivation, feedstock transportation, and CH4 losses (Bacenetti et al., 2013).

Various analytical perspectives are required for a fuller understanding of complex systems, such as a biogas upgrading facility. Economic and environmental analyses can estimate indicators that can be further used for comparison or optimization, such as the cost of a product or the emissions from the system. A techno-economic analysis (TEA) is an analysis tool that evaluates the feasibility of a certain system, breaking down the process for profit optimization (Giacomella, 2021; Mahmud et al., 2021). The minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) is a common estimated parameter from a TEA that can be used for further comparison within clean energy systems (Batan et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2016; Lan et al., 2021; Mahmud et al., 2021). Additionally, a life cycle assessment (LCA) works to quantify the effect a product or process has on the surrounding environment (Góralczyk, 2003). Typical indicators for an LCA include estimation of the global warming potential (GWP) or eutrophication of a system (Abusoglu et al., 2017; Goglio et al., 2012; Xue et al., 2016). The combination of TEA and LCA provides meaningful insights for a system, clarifying areas that may need to be targeted for improvement to assist in further adoption of a technology (Wunderlich et al., 2021).
5: <title>Background </title>

AD is the biological breakdown of organic matter without oxygen present. The product of AD is biogas, largely composed of CH4 and CO2, with trace gases such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (Ward et al., 2008). The biogas stream can be combusted on-site or upgraded further. RNG, the upgraded biogas stream made up almost entirely of CH4, offers the same product as natural gas from sustainable sources such as food waste or biomass. The residue leftover from AD, known as digestate, is dense with nutrients like N and phosphorus (P) and is often applied to crop fields (García-López et al., 2023). The four steps of AD include hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis and methanogenesis, with each step relying on different microbial groups that require unique optimal environmental conditions (Meegoda et al., 2018). Other parameters affecting digestion include the hydraulic retention time, pH, loading rate, and the temperature of the digester (Meegoda et al., 2018). Typical feedstocks of AD include municipal solids waste, food waste, livestock manure, and agricultural residues due to their availability.

Several parts of the world utilize AD, with Germany leading expansion of digestion with over 10,000 projects reported in 2018 (Cai et al., 2021). Within the U.S., there were 343 on-farm AD operations from livestock manure in 2023 (U.S. EPA, 2023b). However, without proper system optimization, AD systems can produce low CH4 yields or have common problems with system wide instability (Wu et al., 2021a). The primary reason cited for biogas operation failure is insufficient profit from low biogas yields (Nguyen et al., 2015). One case study estimated a farm selling electricity from AD lost $10,000 during a two-week shutdown from a system failure (Labatut & Gooch, 2012). Boosting system efficiency optimizes the produced CH4 yields, ultimately leading to an increase in savings. Possible efficiency strategies include increasing
feedstock's bioavailability during digestion or utilizing recycle loops downstream (Wu et al., 2021b).

While materials like manure are inexpensive and readily available, they can often lack the nutrients required for larger gas production (Bardi et al., 2023). Specifically, the carbon to nitrogen ratio (C/N) is often too low within livestock manure, causing issues within the system due to the low amount of energy present (Neshat et al., 2017). Conversely, the C/N ratio of lignocellulosic material, a very complex substrate, is often too high for proper digestion (Neshat et al., 2017). Digesting various feedstocks at the same, known as co-digestion, can provide better nutrients to the microbes and decrease toxicity (Siddique & Wahid, 2018). Co-digestion is typically recommended for efficiency and stability purposes (Bhatt & Tao, 2020). However, this feedstock integration can lead to an increase in price if more costly feedstocks are used, like energy crops. Velásquez Piñas et al. (2019) determined the mono-digestion of cattle manure to be more cost effective for smaller sized plants, while co-digestion was beneficial for larger scale digestion systems, where over 1000 kWe is produced (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

Another approach to increase bioavailability of feedstocks is to include a pretreatment process. This is a common approach for lignocellulosic biomass integration into biorefineries as the material is often difficult to digest (Kumar et al., 2020). Lignocellulosic biomass consists of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, making up 35-50%, 20-35%, and 5-30% of the material respectively (Kassaye, et al., 2016). The intricate network of hydroxyl groups within the cellulose and the rigid bindings of the three fractions creates a complex substrate for digestion (Mankar et al., 2021). Pretreatment of lignocellulosic material can increase bioavailability by reducing the recalcitrance and opening the structure further, therefore increasing accessibility (Bhutto et al., 2017; Fernandes et al., 2009). A number of pretreatment options for lignin

degradation have been researched such as steam, dilute-acid, or enzymatic pretreatments methods (Galbe & Zacchi, 2012). Matching co-digestion, the addition of pretreatment chemicals, higher system energy requirements, or upstream units can add to environmental impacts and operational costs of the plant (Baruah et al., 2018; Rezania et al., 2020).

To increase the usability of biogas, technologies for upgrading the produced biogas are implemented into the system (Kapoor et al., 2019). H2S and CO2 are usually targeted for removal within upgrading facilities. These removal steps are found to typically remove other trace elements found within the gas stream such as water vapor, ammonia, or siloxane (Salihu & Alam, 2015). The concentrations of these gas contaminants are dependent on the feedstock composition and can become corrosive during combustion if not removed beforehand (Awe et al., 2017; Khan et al., 2021). H2S is toxic and corrosive, typically found in levels of 100 ppm to 10,000 ppm within biogas and can negatively affect downstream units such as boilers and CO2 towers (Atelge et al., 2021, Abatzoglou & Boivin, 2008).

Depending on biogas use, the gas stream can be partitioned for combustion or for CO2 removal after H2S removal. Even without upgrading, biogas combusted on-site for electricity still has environmental improvements when compared to conventional electricity, reducing the resources used or CO2 equivalence emitted when compared to conventional electricity (de Meester, et al., 2012; Kaparaju & Rintala, 2011). There are several different methods that can be used for CO2 removal including membranes, pressure swing adsorption, and water scrubbers (Petersson & Wellinger, 2009). While gas upgrading does increase energy density and therefore optimizes the potential for uses, gas upgrading has large capital costs that act as a barrier for further implementation (Vasco-Correa et al., 2018).

Vo et al. (2018) completed an economic analysis on various CO2 separation technologies from the digestion of grass silage and dairy manure, determining amine scrubbing to be more cost effective than biological methanation. With CO2 removed, the remaining biomethane can be used for several applications including energy for fuel cells, transportational use, or industrial purposes (Andriani et al., 2014). Injection of RNG into a natural gas pipeline is a major capital cost, requiring even more capital for upgrading (Peppers et al., 2019). Other factors influencing the environmental impact of an AD system include biomass cultivation, feedstock transportation, and CH4 losses (Bacenetti et al., 2013).

Various analytical perspectives are required for a fuller understanding of complex systems, such as a biogas upgrading facility. Economic and environmental analyses can estimate indicators that can be further used for comparison or optimization, such as the cost of a product or the emissions from the system. A techno-economic analysis (TEA) is an analysis tool that evaluates the feasibility of a certain system, breaking down the process for profit optimization (Giacomella, 2021; Mahmud et al., 2021). The minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) is a common estimated parameter from a TEA that can be used for further comparison within clean energy systems (Batan et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2016; Lan et al., 2021; Mahmud et al., 2021). Additionally, a life cycle assessment (LCA) works to quantify the effect a product or process has on the surrounding environment (Góralczyk, 2003). Typical indicators for an LCA include estimation of the global warming potential (GWP) or eutrophication of a system (Abusoglu et al., 2017; Goglio et al., 2012; Xue et al., 2016). The combination of TEA and LCA provides meaningful insights for a system, clarifying areas that may need to be targeted for improvement to assist in further adoption of a technology (Wunderlich et al., 2021).

0: <title>Thesis Overview </title>

Chapter Two consists of a paper in preparation for submission to GCB Bioenergy, "Techno-economic and environmental assessment of converting mixed prairie to renewable natural gas with co-product hydroxycinnamic acid." This paper analyzes a pretreatment method for prairie biomass and the collection of a co-product. The primary author and researcher of this paper is Katherine Wild, with co-authors Elmin Rahic, Lisa A. Schulte, and Mark Mba Wright. Chapter Three consists of a paper in preparation for journal submission to Biofpr "Integration of Biogas Model (ADM1) for Economic and Environmental Analysis of RNG Production." The primary author and researcher of this paper is Katherine Wild, with co-authors Juan Carlos Domínguez Toribio, Lisa A. Schulte, and Mark Mba Wright.

0: <title>CHAPTER 2. TECHNO-ECONOMIC AND ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSMENT OF CONVERTING MIXED PRAIRIE TO RENEWABLE NATURAL GAS WITH CO- PRODUCT HYDROXYCINNAMIC ACID </title>
Katherine Wild1, Elmin Rahic1, Lisa A. Schulte1, Mark Mba Wright
[1] Iowa State University, Ames, IA 50011, United States
1: <header>Abstract </header>

Investigation into clean alternative energy sources is needed to meet future energy demands. Renewable natural gas (RNG) can assist in meeting growing demand projections of natural gas in a sustainable manner. This study focuses on a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) for the co-digestion of manure with pretreated prairie biomass. This process offers higher biogas yields and an additional co-product, hydroxycinnamic acid (HCA), to decrease the minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) of RNG. The plant produces between 524,000 GJ/yr and 1,176,000 GJ/yr of RNG in the five modeled scenarios. The MFSP for the scenario where no pretreatment occurred is estimated to be $21.2/GJ. Due to the efficiency increase and the co-product collection, the pretreatment process lowered the MFSP to between $15.9/GJ to $18.9/GJ. The global warming potential (GWP) when no pretreatment occurred was estimated at -7.3 kg CO2e/GJ and increased to as high as 21.6 kg CO2e/GJ when pretreatment occurred due to use of additional resources. Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were also completed.
2: <header>Introduction </header>

To reach carbon neutrality by 2050 the U.S. must consider the use of various pathways to meet growing energy demands, projected to grow anywhere from 0.5% to 1.6% yearly until 2050 (U.S. EIA, 2023a). Utilization of technologies such as anaerobic digestion (AD) can avoid

emissions into the atmosphere from the degradation of feedstocks, such as manure, while providing sustainable energy products (Bracmort, 2010). Renewable natural gas (RNG) from AD has facilitated the reduction of greenhouse gases (GHG) while also increasing energy security and diversification through existing infrastructure, such as pipelines (Budzianowski & Brodacka, 2017; Shanmugam et al., 2018; U.S. EPA., 2024).

AD is a biological process that facilitates the conversion of organic matter to biogas in the absence of oxygen. The biogas stream is typically composed of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) along with trace gases such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (Ward et al., 2008). Captured biogas from AD can be burned on-site for heat and electricity generation or upgraded further into RNG via CH4 separation. Digestate, the residue leftover from AD, has attractive properties for soil application such as the capacity to increase nutrient availability within the soil (Fernández-Bayo et al., 2017).

Several factors affect feedstock choice for AD, such as availability, digestibility, nutrient composition, and the presence of inhibitors (Hadin & Eriksson, 2016). While waste feedstocks such as manure are inexpensive, readily available, reduce GHG emissions, and contain beneficial microbes, they typically have low biodegradability leading to limited gas production rates (Jasinska et al., 2023). Co-digestion is considered the easiest and least expensive way to increase AD efficiency, as it may improve system robustness and CH4 yields (Jasióska et al., 2023; Karki et al., 2021).

The utilization of lignocellulosic biomass, a carbon-rich substrate composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin for fuels has been well explored, primarily through the conversion of corn stover to ethanol. There is a large potential of lignocellulosic feedstocks from agro- industrial sources and cultivation of energy crops such as switchgrass (Panicum virgatum L.)

(Ahmed et al., 2019). Cultivation of perennial grasses has several ecological benefits such as water quality improvement, soil erosion reduction, and increased carbon sequestration when compared to traditional row crops (McLaughlin et al., 2002). Song et al. (2023) reports perennial energy grasses to be an ideal renewable energy source for AD due to their high conversion efficiency and low production costs. However, the rigid structure of lignocellulosic biomass makes microbial and enzymatic degradation difficult (Hashemi et al., 2021; Zoghlami & Paes, 2019). This is largely due to the presence of lignin, which binds cellulose and hemicellulose within the cell wall, acting as a barrier against microbes (Zoghlami & Paês, 2019).

While it is common practice with AD systems to pretreat raw feedstocks through mechanical, chemical, biological, or thermal means, pretreatment is even more relevant for biomass with a high lignin content. For example, Xie et al. (2011) tested the effect of temperature and sodium hydroxide loading rates for the pretreatment of dried grass silage, reporting an increase in CH4 yields between 10% to 39%. Dell'Omo & Spena (2020) found that mechanically pretreating giant reed straw in a two-stage milling process could improve CH4 production by 137.7%.

Alkaline pretreatment has specifically been revered for lignocellulosic biomass due to its selectivity of targeting lignin without carbohydrate degradation within a relatively straightforward process (Kim et al., 2016). Johnston et al. (2020) investigated hydroxycinnamic acid (HCA) extraction from corn stover via alkaline pretreatment to produce ethanol. The extracted HCA (p-coumaric and ferulic acid) served as a high-value co-product to improve the economic viability of the cellulosic ethanol plant. This pretreatment process was reported to reduce the minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) of ethanol by $0.26/L, due to improvements in glucose availability and the addition of HCA.

In this study, we assess the comparative cost and environmental performance of collection of novel co-product, HCA, from the pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass for RNG production. We utilize processes and yields from an experimental study completed by Rahic et al. (In Preparation), with the respective consent form shown in Appendix C. The experiments involved the alkaline pretreatment of prairie biomass to break down lignin and recover HCA. We extend their study by performing a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) to determine the impact of prairie biomass pretreatment and HCA recovery. We examine five scenarios, and associated sensitivity and uncertainty analyses, for potential strategies to produce RNG in a profitable and sustainable manner.

Johnston et al. (2020) investigated hydroxycinnamic acid (HCA) extraction from corn stover via alkaline pretreatment to produce ethanol. The extracted HCA (p-coumaric and ferulic acid) served as a high-value co-product to improve the economic viability of the cellulosic ethanol plant. This pretreatment process was reported to reduce the minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) of ethanol by $0.26/L, due to improvements in glucose availability and the addition of HCA.

In this study, we assess the comparative cost and environmental performance of collection of novel co-product, HCA, from the pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass for RNG production. We utilize processes and yields from an experimental study completed by Rahic et al. (In Preparation), with the respective consent form shown in Appendix C. The experiments involved the alkaline pretreatment of prairie biomass to break down lignin and recover HCA. We extend their study by performing a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) to determine the impact of prairie biomass pretreatment and HCA recovery. We examine five scenarios, and associated sensitivity and uncertainty analyses, for potential strategies to produce RNG in a profitable and sustainable manner.
4: <header>Introduction </header>

To reach carbon neutrality by 2050 the U.S. must consider the use of various pathways to meet growing energy demands, projected to grow anywhere from 0.5% to 1.6% yearly until 2050 (U.S. EIA, 2023a). Utilization of technologies such as anaerobic digestion (AD) can avoid

emissions into the atmosphere from the degradation of feedstocks, such as manure, while providing sustainable energy products (Bracmort, 2010). Renewable natural gas (RNG) from AD has facilitated the reduction of greenhouse gases (GHG) while also increasing energy security and diversification through existing infrastructure, such as pipelines (Budzianowski & Brodacka, 2017; Shanmugam et al., 2018; U.S. EPA., 2024).

AD is a biological process that facilitates the conversion of organic matter to biogas in the absence of oxygen. The biogas stream is typically composed of methane (CH4) and carbon dioxide (CO2) along with trace gases such as hydrogen sulfide (H2S), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (Ward et al., 2008). Captured biogas from AD can be burned on-site for heat and electricity generation or upgraded further into RNG via CH4 separation. Digestate, the residue leftover from AD, has attractive properties for soil application such as the capacity to increase nutrient availability within the soil (Fernández-Bayo et al., 2017).

Several factors affect feedstock choice for AD, such as availability, digestibility, nutrient composition, and the presence of inhibitors (Hadin & Eriksson, 2016). While waste feedstocks such as manure are inexpensive, readily available, reduce GHG emissions, and contain beneficial microbes, they typically have low biodegradability leading to limited gas production rates (Jasinska et al., 2023). Co-digestion is considered the easiest and least expensive way to increase AD efficiency, as it may improve system robustness and CH4 yields (Jasióska et al., 2023; Karki et al., 2021).

The utilization of lignocellulosic biomass, a carbon-rich substrate composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin for fuels has been well explored, primarily through the conversion of corn stover to ethanol. There is a large potential of lignocellulosic feedstocks from agro- industrial sources and cultivation of energy crops such as switchgrass (Panicum virgatum L.)

(Ahmed et al., 2019). Cultivation of perennial grasses has several ecological benefits such as water quality improvement, soil erosion reduction, and increased carbon sequestration when compared to traditional row crops (McLaughlin et al., 2002). Song et al. (2023) reports perennial energy grasses to be an ideal renewable energy source for AD due to their high conversion efficiency and low production costs. However, the rigid structure of lignocellulosic biomass makes microbial and enzymatic degradation difficult (Hashemi et al., 2021; Zoghlami & Paes, 2019). This is largely due to the presence of lignin, which binds cellulose and hemicellulose within the cell wall, acting as a barrier against microbes (Zoghlami & Paês, 2019).

While it is common practice with AD systems to pretreat raw feedstocks through mechanical, chemical, biological, or thermal means, pretreatment is even more relevant for biomass with a high lignin content. For example, Xie et al. (2011) tested the effect of temperature and sodium hydroxide loading rates for the pretreatment of dried grass silage, reporting an increase in CH4 yields between 10% to 39%. Dell'Omo & Spena (2020) found that mechanically pretreating giant reed straw in a two-stage milling process could improve CH4 production by 137.7%.

Alkaline pretreatment has specifically been revered for lignocellulosic biomass due to its selectivity of targeting lignin without carbohydrate degradation within a relatively straightforward process (Kim et al., 2016). Johnston et al. (2020) investigated hydroxycinnamic acid (HCA) extraction from corn stover via alkaline pretreatment to produce ethanol. The extracted HCA (p-coumaric and ferulic acid) served as a high-value co-product to improve the economic viability of the cellulosic ethanol plant. This pretreatment process was reported to reduce the minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) of ethanol by $0.26/L, due to improvements in glucose availability and the addition of HCA.

In this study, we assess the comparative cost and environmental performance of collection of novel co-product, HCA, from the pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass for RNG production. We utilize processes and yields from an experimental study completed by Rahic et al. (In Preparation), with the respective consent form shown in Appendix C. The experiments involved the alkaline pretreatment of prairie biomass to break down lignin and recover HCA. We extend their study by performing a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) to determine the impact of prairie biomass pretreatment and HCA recovery. We examine five scenarios, and associated sensitivity and uncertainty analyses, for potential strategies to produce RNG in a profitable and sustainable manner.

7: We utilized version 2.38.6 of BioSTEAM in Python 3.11 (Python Software Foundation, 2022, Cortes-Peña) for our process model, using available, modified, and created units. The system starts with the pretreatment of prairie biomass for co-digestion with beef cattle manure, based on experimental results (Rahic et al., In Preparation). The biogas cleaning process begins by H2S removal from the collected biogas, allowing for partial biogas combustion. CH4 separation is completed via an amine scrubber system based on the design outlined by Skorek- Osikowska et al. (2020). Finally, pyrolysis is performed on the solid digestate to produce biochar as a soil amendment. The full process design is shown in Figure 2.1.

Figure 2.1: Full process diagram of upgrading biogas from prairie biomass and manure to RNG. The rectangles indicate units that are not present in all scenarios. The solid rectangle surrounds the pretreatment process present in scenarios PreHCA, Pre25HCA, Pre50HCA, and PreBHCA. The dotted rectangle surrounds the biochar added to the digester in PreBHCA. See Table 1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

9: <header>Scenarios </header>

In this study, we utilized the experimental process and results of Rahic et al. (In Preparation), where prairie biomass and beef cattle manure were co-digested under five combinations of pretreatment and AD processes. The prairie biomass used in Rahic et al. (In Preparation) was composed of a mixture of dried grass and forb plant species harvested from Iowa State University Agricultural Engineering and Agronomy Research Farm, located in Boone County, IA, USA. Over a 9-year period, 59 species of prairie biomass have been recorded in this location (Kordbacheh et al., 2018). The manure was collected from a cattle producer located in Kingsley, IA. We examined five scenarios within this study being Control, PreHCA, Pre25HCA, Pre50HCA, and PreBHCA, all producing RNG and digestate-derived biochar. All pretreatment scenarios produce HCA. Table 2.1 lists all scenario differences.

Table 2.1: Scenario differences for the co-digestion of prairie biomass and manure, following experimental results from Rahic et al. (In Preparation). Percent liquid recirculation values are from experimental setup and are not considered for the design of this system.

Scenario|Prairie biomass pretreatment via alkaline solution|HCA collection|Percent liquid recirculation|Biochar addition to the digester Control|No|No|0|No PreHCA|Yes|Yes|0|No Pre25HCA|Yes|Yes|25|No Pre50HCA|Yes|Yes|50|No PreBHCA|Yes|Yes|0|Yes

11: <header>Plant Size </header>

di Perta et al. (2019) conducted a TEA on an existing AD plant collecting manure from a dairy farm housing 150 cattle and 200 buffalo to determine if upgrading biogas into RNG would be profitable; ultimately finding it was infeasible due to high investment costs. Another case study located in Idaho determined upgrading biogas to RNG to be economically feasible with herds larger than 3700. The largest net present value (NPV) found was associated with the largest herd analyzed at 8000 (Lauer et al., 2018). As literature favors larger herd sizes, we are assuming this facility is collecting manure from a location holding 5000 beef cattle, as Uddin et al. (2022) also assumed on a TEA examining RNG from beef cattle manure. Each animal is assumed to produce 45 kg/d of manure, with each animal averaging 540 kg (Hammack & Gill, 2016, James et al., 2006).
12: <header>Feedstocks </header>

Both feedstock's characteristics (Table 2.2) are coupled with available laboratory data and the Phyllis2 database, created by the Energy Research Center of the Netherlands (ECN Phyllis2, 2023). All cellulose and hemicellulose present in the system are represented as glucose and xylose, respectively. The prairie biomass arrives at the facility dried while the manure is
pumped in from a closed storage tank in a nearby location. The prairie biomass to manure ratio enters the digester at a 2:1 gVS/gVS basis per the laboratory procedure.
Table 2.2: Feedstock composition and parameters from previous experimental work (Rahic et al., In Preparation) denoted by *, , otherwise determined by ECN Phyllis2 (2023).

Feedstock Composition|Prairie Biomass|Beef Cattle Manure Glucose (wt.%)|32.7|5.61 Xylose (wt.%)|28.0|4.09 Lignin (wt.%)|19.1|4.61 Water* (wt.%)|6.10|82.8 Ash (wt.%)|5.84|2.86 Extractives (wt.%)|8.34|0 Sulfur (wt.%)|0|0.0316 Volatile Solids * (wt.%)|86.0|14.5
Full Process
13: PreHCA, Pre25HCA, Pre50HCA, and PreBHCA pretreat shredded prairie biomass with a solution made up of 50% ethanol, 30% water, and 20% potassium hydroxide (KOH) to facilitate the breakdown of lignin and sequester HCA. We reduced the laboratory ratio of pretreatment solution to prairie biomass to 11 mL/g to save on costs and materials (Johnston et al., 2020). The pretreatment process occurs at 80°C for 2 hours in a pretreatment tank with yields identified for p-coumaric and ferulic acid at 21.3 g/kg and 15.1 g/kg of biomass, respectively. The separation of HCA from the solution has a recovery rate of 95%. The modification of BioSTEAM's mix tank creates the pretreatment reactor (U1) to allow for heating.
14: <header>Digestion </header>
The anaerobic digester operates as a mesophilic digester with a temperature of 35°C and a retention time of 30 days. We utilize experimental gas production rates from Rahic et al. (In

Preparation) while the digester stream recycling water (S17) make up 10% total solids within the digester to model full-scale digestion accurately. The process yields are based on overall CH4 and biogas yields that varies for each scenario (Table 2.3). The rest of the biogas stream is based on the product composition reported by the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (Humbird et al., 2011). The digestate fraction, on a weight (wt.) basis, consists of 85% water, 6.5% lignin, 3.1% ash, and others. PreBHCA requires the addition of biochar into the digester, making up 5% of the biomass and manure's dry wt. As the laboratory procedure utilized biochar derived from corn stover, the biochar is transported to the facility.

16: Our main goal of the gas cleaning process is to remove H2S within the gas stream, to avoid its corrosive and toxic properties downstream (Andriani et al., 2020). Once collection of all biogas streams (S18, S52) is completed, the combined stream (S19) is compressed for the removal of H2S. The modification of BioSTEAM's mixer makes up the desulfurization unit (U2) to separate any H2S present in the biogas stream into hydrogen gas and sulfur.
17: The boiler unit (B) combusts 5% of the cleaned biogas stream along with purchased natural gas to provide heat and electricity to the system (S44, S45).

The separation of CH4 from biogas is completed to produce the RNG stream (S35). This separation step utilizes absorber (A1) and stripper (A2) technology, where BioSTEAM's shortcut column is used. CO2 is separated from CH4 using monoethanolamine (MEA), which removes CO2 molecules in the absorber column at a rate of 95%. The MEA stream flows into the system (S25) at a 1:1 molar basis (Huertas et al., 2015). The stripper column then separates out MEA from CO2 for amine recycling at 95%. Additional flashing (F1, F2) is completed for further
separation of CO2. The CH4 stream is injected into a natural gas pipeline with a 12.7-cm diameter and a connection point of 3.21 km.
18: This section aims to process an additional co-product of biochar as a soil amendment. The biosolids stream (S50) out of the digester is flashed (F3) for additional separation of biogas. The residue is dried to a final moisture content of 10% for eventual pyrolysis, where biochar is produced at an 80% yield for all non-water components. The biochar is recovered at a rate of 99% (S58). This section also includes a wastewater treatment system (W).
19: BioSTEAM is utilized for completion of the TEA with modification of BioSTEAM's model assumptions. Modification of the digester's capital costs allow the cost to be dependent on the total flow of biogas out of the unit. The modification of the prairie biomass shredder's cost is dependent on the flow into the system while the modified compressor's cost relies on the total flow of gas out. The pipeline's capital costs were based on Brown et al. (2022), accounting for material, labor, right-of-way, and miscellaneous costs for the specified pipeline.
20: <header>Operating Costs </header>

The sum of fixed and variable costs within a system makes up the total operating costs. Variable costs are determined by levels of production within the plant, such as feedstock or utility costs while fixed costs are independent from plant output and will remain constant, such as maintenance or insurance costs of the biorefinery. Table 2.4 shows feedstock and chemical costs. Each scenario's MFSP is estimated at the NPV break-even point.

Manure handling costs are $30/tonne as literature prices ranged from $3/tonne to $95/tonne (Bentley et al., 2014; Dennis, 2022; Howland & Karszes, 2012). Prairie biomass costs range extensively. Perrin & Harlow (2019) state that while the price of switchgrass, a plant species commonly found in prairie mixtures, sold to biorefineries is not yet known, the price will at least have to match the livestock feed value at $104.6/tonne of dry matter. Bangsund et al. (2008) completed a study of the cost of switchgrass production and profitability and determined switchgrass to have a breakeven farm-gate price ranging from $61.68/tonne to $105.7/tonne, depending on soil productivity. The price of prairie biomass was ultimately assumed as $105/tonne and includes transportation costs to the plant.

Biochar pricing also ranged, as Campion et al. (2023) found prices for biochar within literature to be as high as $2710/tonne and as low as $17.2/tonne. Biochar is sold at $200/tonne, as Thengane et al. (2021) reports for the minimum price of production within California. All chemical costs including p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, ethanol, MEA, and KOH solids came from Alibaba (Alibaba). The natural gas price was selected from the U.S. average in 2023 from the Henry Hub natural gas pipeline (U.S. EIA, 2023)
Table 2.4: Assigned prices of feedstocks, chemicals, and products for all scenarios.

Product|Price Biochar ($/tonne)|200 p-Coumaric Acid ($/kg)|8 Ferulic Acid ($/kg)|4

The internal rate of return (IRR) is a site-specific parameter and is typically dependent on the costs and income of a project (Ghose & Franchetti, 2018). Hublin et al. (2014) performed a TEA on the co-digestion of cow manure and whey, targeting IRRs at 4.2%, 6.6%, and 7.1% under different effectuation periods. Aui et al. (2019) investigated an AD plant located in Iowa with a plant life of 30 years and determined effective IRRs to be between 3.51% to 5.57% for six scenarios. For this study, the IRR was set at 5% for a project lifespan of 20 years. The labor cost was obtained from the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy's Labor Cost Estimator Mini-Tool (2022) along with Salary.com to determine wages of biogas workers.

Fixed capital investment (FCI) is calculated by applying the lang factor, a tool used to relate the FCI to the total purchasing cost, originally proposed to range from 3 to 5 (Sinnott, 1996). Amigun & von Blottniz (2009) determined a biogas facility located in Sub-Saharan Africa to have a lang factor of 2.63. A system upgrading biogas with an amine scrubber had a lang factor of 5 (Gawel, 2012). A pilot study processing lignocellulosic biomass for ethanol assumed a lang factor of 3.2 (van Rijn et al., 2018). Here, our lang factor is assumed as 2.7 after taking relevant literature into account. Depreciation periods of biorefineries with AD technology employed typically range from 5-7 years (Aui et al., 2019; Naqi et al, 2019; Uddin et al., 2022). The depreciation period of this plant was set at 7 years using the modified accelerated cost
recovery system. All other economic assumptions are determined by Davis et al. (2016). Our economic assumptions can be seen in Table 2.5.
Table 2.5: Economic assumptions for the TEA of RNG production. Several parameters are percentages of FCI and total capital cost (TCC).

Parameters|Input Value IRR (%)|5 Duration of Project|2018-2038 Depreciation (yr)|7 Combined State and Federal Income Tax (%)|21 Operating Time (d/yr)|333 Lang Factor|2.7 Construction Schedule (year 0 %, year 1 %)|40, 60 Working Capital over FCI (%)|5 Labor Cost ($/yr)|84,850 Fringe Benefits (labor cost %)|40 Property Tax (FCI %)|0.1 Property Insurance (FCI%)|0.5 Supplies (labor cost %)|2 Maintenance (FCI %)|5 Administration (FCI %)|0.5 Finance Fraction (TCC%)|40 Finance Time (yr)|10 Finance Interest (TCC %)|7
We also explored various economic incentives for carbon reduction. Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, awarded by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency

under the Renewable Fuel Standard Program, are tradable credits created when a fuel reducing GHGs is produced. Biofuels from cellulosic feedstocks fall under the D3 category, requiring a GHG reduction of 60% when compared to the fossil fuel pathway it is replacing. All D3 RIN pathways recorded in 2022 by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency averaged to be $3.07/credit (U.S. EPA, 2023b).

An additional economic incentive investigated is the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) credits awarded by California Air Resources Board. LCFS credits rely on a determined carbon intensity (CI) score, awarded for reduction of GHGs for transportation fuels. A CI score is determined to be the amount of GHGs emitted from burning a fuel, typically considered the life cycle of a fuel measured in CO2 equivalence (CO2e). While there have been several approved pathways of compressed natural gas from dairy manure with CI scores ranging from -533 to - -133 g CO2e/MJ, there is currently no approved pathway for beef cattle manure (California Air Resources Board, 2023a). With the addition of prairie biomass as an energy crop, the CI score is expected to increase for this study. As of November 2023, the LCFS credit averaged $71.6/MT (California Air Resources Board, 2023a).
21: <header>Operating Costs </header>

The sum of fixed and variable costs within a system makes up the total operating costs. Variable costs are determined by levels of production within the plant, such as feedstock or utility costs while fixed costs are independent from plant output and will remain constant, such as maintenance or insurance costs of the biorefinery. Table 2.4 shows feedstock and chemical costs. Each scenario's MFSP is estimated at the NPV break-even point.

Manure handling costs are $30/tonne as literature prices ranged from $3/tonne to $95/tonne (Bentley et al., 2014; Dennis, 2022; Howland & Karszes, 2012). Prairie biomass costs range extensively. Perrin & Harlow (2019) state that while the price of switchgrass, a plant species commonly found in prairie mixtures, sold to biorefineries is not yet known, the price will at least have to match the livestock feed value at $104.6/tonne of dry matter. Bangsund et al. (2008) completed a study of the cost of switchgrass production and profitability and determined switchgrass to have a breakeven farm-gate price ranging from $61.68/tonne to $105.7/tonne, depending on soil productivity. The price of prairie biomass was ultimately assumed as $105/tonne and includes transportation costs to the plant.

Biochar pricing also ranged, as Campion et al. (2023) found prices for biochar within literature to be as high as $2710/tonne and as low as $17.2/tonne. Biochar is sold at $200/tonne, as Thengane et al. (2021) reports for the minimum price of production within California. All chemical costs including p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, ethanol, MEA, and KOH solids came from Alibaba (Alibaba). The natural gas price was selected from the U.S. average in 2023 from the Henry Hub natural gas pipeline (U.S. EIA, 2023)

The internal rate of return (IRR) is a site-specific parameter and is typically dependent on the costs and income of a project (Ghose & Franchetti, 2018). Hublin et al. (2014) performed a TEA on the co-digestion of cow manure and whey, targeting IRRs at 4.2%, 6.6%, and 7.1% under different effectuation periods. Aui et al. (2019) investigated an AD plant located in Iowa with a plant life of 30 years and determined effective IRRs to be between 3.51% to 5.57% for six scenarios. For this study, the IRR was set at 5% for a project lifespan of 20 years. The labor cost was obtained from the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy's Labor Cost Estimator Mini-Tool (2022) along with Salary.com to determine wages of biogas workers.

Fixed capital investment (FCI) is calculated by applying the lang factor, a tool used to relate the FCI to the total purchasing cost, originally proposed to range from 3 to 5 (Sinnott, 1996). Amigun & von Blottniz (2009) determined a biogas facility located in Sub-Saharan Africa to have a lang factor of 2.63. A system upgrading biogas with an amine scrubber had a lang factor of 5 (Gawel, 2012). A pilot study processing lignocellulosic biomass for ethanol assumed a lang factor of 3.2 (van Rijn et al., 2018). Here, our lang factor is assumed as 2.7 after taking relevant literature into account. Depreciation periods of biorefineries with AD technology employed typically range from 5-7 years (Aui et al., 2019; Naqi et al, 2019; Uddin et al., 2022). The depreciation period of this plant was set at 7 years using the modified accelerated cost
recovery system. All other economic assumptions are determined by Davis et al. (2016). Our economic assumptions can be seen in Table 2.5.
Table 2.5: Economic assumptions for the TEA of RNG production. Several parameters are percentages of FCI and total capital cost (TCC).

We also explored various economic incentives for carbon reduction. Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits, awarded by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency

under the Renewable Fuel Standard Program, are tradable credits created when a fuel reducing GHGs is produced. Biofuels from cellulosic feedstocks fall under the D3 category, requiring a GHG reduction of 60% when compared to the fossil fuel pathway it is replacing. All D3 RIN pathways recorded in 2022 by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency averaged to be $3.07/credit (U.S. EPA, 2023b).

An additional economic incentive investigated is the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) credits awarded by California Air Resources Board. LCFS credits rely on a determined carbon intensity (CI) score, awarded for reduction of GHGs for transportation fuels. A CI score is determined to be the amount of GHGs emitted from burning a fuel, typically considered the life cycle of a fuel measured in CO2 equivalence (CO2e). While there have been several approved pathways of compressed natural gas from dairy manure with CI scores ranging from -533 to - -133 g CO2e/MJ, there is currently no approved pathway for beef cattle manure (California Air Resources Board, 2023a). With the addition of prairie biomass as an energy crop, the CI score is expected to increase for this study. As of November 2023, the LCFS credit averaged $71.6/MT (California Air Resources Board, 2023a).
26: <header>Operating Costs </header>

The sum of fixed and variable costs within a system makes up the total operating costs. Variable costs are determined by levels of production within the plant, such as feedstock or utility costs while fixed costs are independent from plant output and will remain constant, such as maintenance or insurance costs of the biorefinery. Table 2.4 shows feedstock and chemical costs. Each scenario's MFSP is estimated at the NPV break-even point.

Manure handling costs are $30/tonne as literature prices ranged from $3/tonne to $95/tonne (Bentley et al., 2014; Dennis, 2022; Howland & Karszes, 2012). Prairie biomass costs range extensively. Perrin & Harlow (2019) state that while the price of switchgrass, a plant species commonly found in prairie mixtures, sold to biorefineries is not yet known, the price will at least have to match the livestock feed value at $104.6/tonne of dry matter. Bangsund et al. (2008) completed a study of the cost of switchgrass production and profitability and determined switchgrass to have a breakeven farm-gate price ranging from $61.68/tonne to $105.7/tonne, depending on soil productivity. The price of prairie biomass was ultimately assumed as $105/tonne and includes transportation costs to the plant.

Biochar pricing also ranged, as Campion et al. (2023) found prices for biochar within literature to be as high as $2710/tonne and as low as $17.2/tonne. Biochar is sold at $200/tonne, as Thengane et al. (2021) reports for the minimum price of production within California. All chemical costs including p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, ethanol, MEA, and KOH solids came from Alibaba (Alibaba). The natural gas price was selected from the U.S. average in 2023 from the Henry Hub natural gas pipeline (U.S. EIA, 2023)

28: LCA is an established tool for evaluating a technology's environmental impact. We completed a cradle-to-gate LCA using the framework of ISO 14,040 and ISO 14,044 (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The system boundary is shown in Figure 2.2, which incorporates the system boundary expansion method to include biochar application. The functional unit is defined as 1 GJ of RNG. GREET software version 1.3.0.13991 was adopted for relevant databases (Argonne National Laboratory, 2023). GREET's global warming potential (GWP) for a 100-year period are
based on the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's sixth assessment, where CH4 and N2O emissions are respectively equivalent to 30 kg CO2e/kg and 273 kg CO2e/kg emitted.

Additional pathways using pre-existing resources were created within GREET to quantify prairie biomass cultivation with diesel, phosphorus (P), potassium (K), and N fertilizer and avoided beef cattle manure emissions (Fazio & Monti, 2011; Gao et al., 2014). Each GJ of prairie biomass requires 0.04 kg of P, 0.20 kg of K, and 0.34 kg of N. GREET's existing pathways for KOH and ethanol were used, and methyl amine substituted MEA. A GWP value for vanillin production was used as an alternative to HCA within GREET, as ferulic acid to vanillin is a known pathway used for many applications (Kumar & Pruthi, 2014). The vanillin was manufactured by Borregaard AS from Scandinavian spruce (The Norwegian EPD Foundation, 2016). A CH4 leak at a rate of 3% of total RNG collected on a mass basis is assumed. The boundary does not include end of life of RNG or chemical loss. All CO2 from combustion of biogas within the system is considered biogenic. All boiler chemicals were found to be insignificant to LCA results and therefore were not included.

30: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>
Boundary and Scope

LCA is an established tool for evaluating a technology's environmental impact. We completed a cradle-to-gate LCA using the framework of ISO 14,040 and ISO 14,044 (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The system boundary is shown in Figure 2.2, which incorporates the system boundary expansion method to include biochar application. The functional unit is defined as 1 GJ of RNG. GREET software version 1.3.0.13991 was adopted for relevant databases (Argonne National Laboratory, 2023). GREET's global warming potential (GWP) for a 100-year period are
based on the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's sixth assessment, where CH4 and N2O emissions are respectively equivalent to 30 kg CO2e/kg and 273 kg CO2e/kg emitted.

Additional pathways using pre-existing resources were created within GREET to quantify prairie biomass cultivation with diesel, phosphorus (P), potassium (K), and N fertilizer and avoided beef cattle manure emissions (Fazio & Monti, 2011; Gao et al., 2014). Each GJ of prairie biomass requires 0.04 kg of P, 0.20 kg of K, and 0.34 kg of N. GREET's existing pathways for KOH and ethanol were used, and methyl amine substituted MEA. A GWP value for vanillin production was used as an alternative to HCA within GREET, as ferulic acid to vanillin is a known pathway used for many applications (Kumar & Pruthi, 2014). The vanillin was manufactured by Borregaard AS from Scandinavian spruce (The Norwegian EPD Foundation, 2016). A CH4 leak at a rate of 3% of total RNG collected on a mass basis is assumed. The boundary does not include end of life of RNG or chemical loss. All CO2 from combustion of biogas within the system is considered biogenic. All boiler chemicals were found to be insignificant to LCA results and therefore were not included.

31: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>
Boundary and Scope

LCA is an established tool for evaluating a technology's environmental impact. We completed a cradle-to-gate LCA using the framework of ISO 14,040 and ISO 14,044 (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The system boundary is shown in Figure 2.2, which incorporates the system boundary expansion method to include biochar application. The functional unit is defined as 1 GJ of RNG. GREET software version 1.3.0.13991 was adopted for relevant databases (Argonne National Laboratory, 2023). GREET's global warming potential (GWP) for a 100-year period are
based on the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's sixth assessment, where CH4 and N2O emissions are respectively equivalent to 30 kg CO2e/kg and 273 kg CO2e/kg emitted.

Additional pathways using pre-existing resources were created within GREET to quantify prairie biomass cultivation with diesel, phosphorus (P), potassium (K), and N fertilizer and avoided beef cattle manure emissions (Fazio & Monti, 2011; Gao et al., 2014). Each GJ of prairie biomass requires 0.04 kg of P, 0.20 kg of K, and 0.34 kg of N. GREET's existing pathways for KOH and ethanol were used, and methyl amine substituted MEA. A GWP value for vanillin production was used as an alternative to HCA within GREET, as ferulic acid to vanillin is a known pathway used for many applications (Kumar & Pruthi, 2014). The vanillin was manufactured by Borregaard AS from Scandinavian spruce (The Norwegian EPD Foundation, 2016). A CH4 leak at a rate of 3% of total RNG collected on a mass basis is assumed. The boundary does not include end of life of RNG or chemical loss. All CO2 from combustion of biogas within the system is considered biogenic. All boiler chemicals were found to be insignificant to LCA results and therefore were not included.

32: <header>Direct and Avoided Emissions </header>

In this study, we assigned several displacement credits. All scenarios producing HCA are displacing HCA embodied emissions elsewhere. Biochar application has associated CO2 and N2O emissions while displacing synthetic N fertilizer on a 1:1 mass basis (Han, et al., 2013). We also considered the impact of biochar application as an alternative to digestate. To assess this impact, we completed a literature review (Table 2.6) to determine the impact thermal treatment has on fixed carbon within digestate. We awarded credits based on the carbon avoidance of digestate application over a 100-year period. Digestate and biochar recalcitrance was assumed to be equivalent to their respective fixed carbon values. Within a 100-year period Han, et al. (2011) reports 84% of the carbon within the digestate applied to soil converts to CO2 due to carbon instability. Biochar's fixed carbon was calculated by subtracting the carbon leaving as py-gas during pyrolysis from digestate's fixed carbon amount. Over a 100-year period, 80% of the carbon from the biochar stays within the soil (Han et al., 2013).

As N makeup within the digestate was determined to not be affected by pyrolysis, both digestate and biochar have an N makeup of 1.5% of the digestate dry weight. Both the N volatized and runoff/leaching as N2O were determined to be 0.01 kg (N2O-N)/kg N (Han et al., 2011). The associated loss factors were based on anaerobic lagoon values of 43% and 0.6% for volatilization and runoff/leaching respectively (Han et al., 2011). Natural gas avoided via biogas combustion was quantified by the negative natural gas emission factor for the methane content in the biogas stream. Appendix A includes additional LCA assumptions. All emission factors (EF) are displayed in Table 2.7.
Table 2.6: Digestate derived biochar literary review. All changes between pre- and post-values are assumed to be solely due to thermal treatment. T denotes temperature.

36: <header>Sensitivity and Uncertainty Analyses </header>

Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses are important for understanding how a change in the parameters within the system affect the conclusions drawn. Both analyses were completed using Python and BioSTEAM. The sensitivity analysis was conducted comparing the base case of several parameters assumed against pessimistic and optimistic versions by +20% to understand how they impact the MFSP of RNG.

Different farm sizes were compared, 4000 head and 6000 head to the base case of 5000 head to determine how the size of the farm impacts the RNG selling price. Various ratios of prairie biomass to manure (1:1 and 3:1) were also assessed against the base case of 2:1 gVS/gVS. This increase in the amount of prairie biomass used could allow for additional ecosystem benefits of growing prairie biomass on smaller farms, without relying on large-scale confined feeding operations for RNG production. As prairie biomass market prices ranged vastly, this parameter was also tested at $65/t and $145/t against the base case of $105/t. Manure costs were also tested at $20/t and $40/t along with HCA prices at $7.5/kg and $4.5/kg. The IRR and equipment installation costs were also tested to determine the impact on RNG's selling price.

The uncertainty analysis evaluated the impact of randomized sampling of key TEA and LCA parameters on the MFSP and GWP estimates. The selected parameters and their ranges are the same as the sensitivity analysis. We employ triangular distributions with minimum and maximums corresponding to the sensitivity ranges, and the average value as the mode of the distributions. We sampled 5000 values for each scenario for full system analysis.

39: <header>Process Modeling Results </header>

RNG production ranges from 524,000 GJ/yr (Control) to 1,175,000 GJ/yr (PreBHCA). Biochar production ranges from 8,670 tonne/yr to 10,730 tonne/yr. All pretreatment scenarios produced 560 tonne/yr and 400 tonne/yr of p-coumaric and ferulic acid, respectively. This pretreatment process decreased the total substrate mass by 19%. The pretreatment process improves the RNG production rate by 87% to 125%. All pretreatment scenarios have similar CH4 and biogas volumes due to the pretreatment of prairie biomass employed, with PreBHCA yielding the most biogas and CH4. Table 2.8 displays the product production rates of each scenario. Figure 2.3 displays the relationship between CH4 and biogas for each scenario.

RNG production rates are proportional to the CH4 and biogas production rates leaving the digester. Comparison of PreHCA, Pre25HCA, and Pre50HCA suggest additional liquid recirculation rates within the system to have insignificant impacts on gas yields. Furthermore, the addition of biochar into the digester results in the highest biogas production rates, presumably because the biochar adsorbs inhibitors, stabilizing the reactor (Saif, et al., 2021). All system flow rates are presented in Appendix B.

40: <header>Process Modeling Results </header>

RNG production ranges from 524,000 GJ/yr (Control) to 1,175,000 GJ/yr (PreBHCA). Biochar production ranges from 8,670 tonne/yr to 10,730 tonne/yr. All pretreatment scenarios produced 560 tonne/yr and 400 tonne/yr of p-coumaric and ferulic acid, respectively. This pretreatment process decreased the total substrate mass by 19%. The pretreatment process improves the RNG production rate by 87% to 125%. All pretreatment scenarios have similar CH4 and biogas volumes due to the pretreatment of prairie biomass employed, with PreBHCA yielding the most biogas and CH4. 42: The facility installed equipment costs range from $29 million to $38 million or about $5800 to $7600 per cattle head. Control has the lowest cost due to the lack of the pretreatment unit and smaller gas processing units. PreBHCA has the highest capital costs. The digester cost varied depending on the rate of recycling and biogas yields, with the digester equipment cost ranging from $2.2 million to $3.3 million. The estimated cost breakdown for the pipeline is due to the material, labor, right-of-way, and miscellaneous costs per 2.54 cm pipe diameter and per 1.61 km pipe length as $24,200, $55,300, $26,400, and $7200, respectively. With a 12.7 cm diameter pipe with 3.22 km of pipe to the connection point, the pipeline costs about $1.13 million. We summarize the equipment cost breakdown in Figure 2.4. The pretreatment process increased the total equipment costs by 27% to 31%. While the pretreatment process increases the annual operating costs by 140%, the yearly sales revenue increases over 200%.
Figure 2.4: Equipment cost breakdown for the five simulated scenarios of prairie biomass and manure co-digestion for RNG. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

Figure 2.5 shows the MFSP for all pretreatment scenarios ($15.9/GJ to $18.9/GJ) to be lower than Control ($21.2/GJ). The increase in CH4 and revenues from HCA for all pretreatment scenarios decreased the MFSP by 11% to 25%. All pretreatment scenarios cost factors are very similar. Manure, prairie biomass, operation and management (O&M), depreciation, and return on investment (ROI) each contribute between $4/GJ and $5/GJ in Control. In the pretreatment scenarios, the contribution from these factors contributes between $2/GJ and $4/GJ due to the increased RNG production rate. The pretreatment scenarios have an additional cost of about $7/GJ (PreBHCA) to $9/GJ (Pre50HCA) for ethanol and KOH, depending on the scenario. All scenarios receive soil amendment revenues of about $3/GJ (Control) or $2/GJ (pretreatment scenarios). The pretreatment scenarios have additional revenues between $4.6/GJ (PreBHCA) and $5.6/GJ (Pre50HCA) from the sale of HCA.

Figure 2.5: Summary of MFSP costs for the five simulated scenarios of prairie biomass and manure co-digestion. The addition of biochar to PreBHCA and all boiler chemical costs are not included in the figure due to their small contribution to the MFSP. Net MFSP are indicated with black boxes. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

46: Figure 2.6 displays the GWP scores for all scenarios. GWP varies from -7.3 to 21.6 kg CO2e/GJ of RNG, with Control having the lowest GWP. The most common source of emissions for all scenarios is the CH4 leak from the system. The pretreatment scenarios' net positive GWP is primarily due to the use of ethanol. Carbon credits from avoided digestate application account for approximately 50% of the negative offset for the total GWP for all scenarios. HCA displacement had little effect on overall GWP, as the displaced HCA was also from bio-based sources. These estimates account for both direct life cycle emissions and indirect emissions such as manure's business-as-usual emission avoidance.

Figure 2.6: Life cycle GWP for five simulated scenarios of prairie biomass and manure co- digestion. The net GWP are indicated with black boxes. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

48: We conducted a sensitivity analysis for each scenario by varying key parameters by 20%. Figure 2.7 shows the MFSP results for all five scenarios. RNG yield has the highest

impact on the MFSP in all the scenarios. The prairie biomass to manure ratio had the second highest impact on Control due to the large difference between the EFs. The ethanol price had the second highest impact on all pretreatment scenarios, due to the amount of ethanol required for the solution. The HCA price was also significant parameter for all pretreatment scenarios. The cattle head number and natural gas price had the smallest impact on the MFSP for all scenarios. The relative importance of these factors could change with different facility configurations. For example, if the facility relies on grid electricity, that parameter's pricing might have a greater impact on the MFSP.
