Figure 2.7: MFSP sensitivity analysis for biomass (PB) and manure (M) co-digestion for RNG. Parameter values vary by 20%. Each scenario has the same key parameters tested, plotted by their respective impact on MFSP. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

The sensitivity of the GWP to various parameters is shown in Figure 2.8. The CH4 leak and the prairie biomass to manure ratio had a large impact on the pretreatment scenarios. Similarly to the MFSP sensitivity analysis, the RNG yield and prairie biomass to manure ratio have a significant impact on the GWP for Control. N2O emissions from biochar had insignificant impacts for all scenarios for the GWP due to the small amount of N2O emitted.

Figure 2.8: GWP sensitivity analysis for biomass (PB) and manure (M) co-digestion for RNG. Parameter values vary by 20%. Each scenario has the same key parameters tested, plotted by their respective impact on GWP. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

The LCA sensitivity analysis results support the need for an integrated process and ecological assessment to better understand the fate of carbon and nutrient flows. There are several factors, such the application of digestate-derived biochar on soil growing prairie biomass that would benefit from further data collection and analysis to reduce the uncertainty in the GWP estimates.

The results of the TEA uncertainty analysis are shown in Figure 2.9. PreBHCA has the lowest MFSP, while Control has the highest. There is almost no overlap between Control and the pretreatment scenarios without liquid recirculation, suggesting these scenarios are statistically significant. On the other hand, there is a significant overlap between the PreHCA and PreBHCA. Therefore, it is not clear that there is a significant advantage in the use of biochar with the pretreatment scenarios. Another key insight is that the pretreatment scenarios have a smaller standard deviation possibly due to the higher RNG yield, which lowers the impact of TEA parameters on the MFSP.

Figure 2.9: MFSP uncertainty analysis for prairie biomass and manure co-digestion for RNG. Parameters are tested from a triangular distribution. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

Figure 2.10 shows the GWP uncertainty analysis results. There is no overlap between Control's GWP and the pretreatment scenarios. Control's GWP ranges from -20 kg CO2e/GJ to 5 kg CO2e/GJ, supporting this scenario as a negative emission strategy. The pretreatment scenarios have emissions ranging from 10 to 30 kg CO2e/GJ. There is almost no difference in the shapes of the pretreatment scenario probability distributions. This suggests that there is no statistical difference in the GWP when using biochar or varying the recycle rate.

Figure 2.10: GWP uncertainty analysis for prairie biomass and manure co-digestion for RNG. Parameters are tested from a triangular distribution. Fossil natural gas GWP has been estimated at 53 kg CO2e/GJ (U.S. EPA, 2023a). See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

Figure 2.7: MFSP sensitivity analysis for biomass (PB) and manure (M) co-digestion for RNG. Parameter values vary by 20%. Each scenario has the same key parameters tested, plotted by their respective impact on MFSP. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

The sensitivity of the GWP to various parameters is shown in Figure 2.8. The CH4 leak and the prairie biomass to manure ratio had a large impact on the pretreatment scenarios. Similarly to the MFSP sensitivity analysis, the RNG yield and prairie biomass to manure ratio have a significant impact on the GWP for Control. N2O emissions from biochar had insignificant impacts for all scenarios for the GWP due to the small amount of N2O emitted.

Figure 2.8: GWP sensitivity analysis for biomass (PB) and manure (M) co-digestion for RNG. Parameter values vary by 20%. Each scenario has the same key parameters tested, plotted by their respective impact on GWP. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

The LCA sensitivity analysis results support the need for an integrated process and ecological assessment to better understand the fate of carbon and nutrient flows. There are several factors, such the application of digestate-derived biochar on soil growing prairie biomass that would benefit from further data collection and analysis to reduce the uncertainty in the GWP estimates.

The results of the TEA uncertainty analysis are shown in Figure 2.9. PreBHCA has the lowest MFSP, while Control has the highest. There is almost no overlap between Control and the pretreatment scenarios without liquid recirculation, suggesting these scenarios are statistically significant. On the other hand, there is a significant overlap between the PreHCA and PreBHCA. Therefore, it is not clear that there is a significant advantage in the use of biochar with the pretreatment scenarios. Another key insight is that the pretreatment scenarios have a smaller standard deviation possibly due to the higher RNG yield, which lowers the impact of TEA parameters on the MFSP.

Figure 2.9: MFSP uncertainty analysis for prairie biomass and manure co-digestion for RNG. Parameters are tested from a triangular distribution. See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

Figure 2.10 shows the GWP uncertainty analysis results. There is no overlap between Control's GWP and the pretreatment scenarios. Control's GWP ranges from -20 kg CO2e/GJ to 5 kg CO2e/GJ, supporting this scenario as a negative emission strategy. The pretreatment scenarios have emissions ranging from 10 to 30 kg CO2e/GJ. There is almost no difference in the shapes of the pretreatment scenario probability distributions. This suggests that there is no statistical difference in the GWP when using biochar or varying the recycle rate.

Figure 2.10: GWP uncertainty analysis for prairie biomass and manure co-digestion for RNG. Parameters are tested from a triangular distribution. Fossil natural gas GWP has been estimated at 53 kg CO2e/GJ (U.S. EPA, 2023a). See Table 2.1 in Materials and Methods section for scenario definition.

56: <header>Discussion </header>

We investigated the feasibility of a pretreatment for prairie biomass to extract HCA co- products and reduce the recalcitrance of biomass for AD. Based on the MFSP, we determined the added costs for pretreatment and larger downstream units are justified by the increased revenue from higher RNG production and HCA generation. Within our study, HCA acts as a low-risk co- product due to utilization of substrate residue from the pretreatment process. Inclusion of diversified co-products within a system can also decrease the risks associated with fuels in addition to lowering the MFSP (Brandt et al., 2020). However, larger gas yields require larger downstream units.

According to switchgrass harvest rates from Kimura et al. (2015), about 1370 ha would be required to cultivate the biomass to feed our facility for one year. Based on the sensitivity analysis, a higher ratio of prairie biomass to manure could further decrease the MFSP. This may

further incentivize prairie biomass generation as some farmers have exhibited hesitation growing cellulosic feedstocks, due partially to concerns of profitability (Fewell et al., 2016). Additionally, there is a growing market for HCA, as Ranjan & Baghel (2023) predicts the Asia Pacific region to have a market value for HCA to be $86 million by 2031, a regional increase of 45% from 2021. HCA can be exploited for many applications within the pharmaceutical, beauty, food, and chemical industries for having admirable traits such as anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, and anti- aging properties (Flourat, et al., 2020; Ranjan & Baghel (2023). Products containing HCA can also earn natural labels while simultaneously promoting a circular economy relying on biomass cultivation (Flourat et al., 2020).

Nelissen et al. (2020) completed a literature review of RNG prices, stating current prices of RNG range $18/GJ to $46/GJ, while prices are projected to lower to $14/GJ to $20/GJ by 2050. Between 2014 and 2022, U.S. liquified natural gas export prices ranged from $5.2/GJ and $15.8/GJ (U.S. EIA 2023). Thus, the current scenarios would need public incentives to compete economically with natural gas. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Renewable Fuels Standard offers RIN credits valued at $1.43/gal of renewable fuel generated (U.S. EPA, 2023b). In 2023, cellulosic biofuel RINS have the highest value ranging from $2/RIN to $3/RIN ($25/GJ to $37/GJ).

The California Air Resources Board determined in just one year a typical livestock farm receives $865,000 from LCFS credits alone (Lee & Sumner, 2018). California's LCFS offers credits that range from $9.5/GJ to $42.6/GJ (California Air Resources Board, 2023b), depending on the technology's CI score. For a CI score of -7 g CO2e/MJ, the 2022 calculated credit is $9.15/GJ, while the calculated credit is $6.69/GJ for a 20 g CO2e/MJ score (California Air Resources Board, 2023b). The LCFS average credit price of 2022 was $121.1/t CO2e (California

Air Resources Board, 2023a). Thus, a combination of RINS and LCFS credit would make this technology economically competitive in the U.S. While the financial incentives associated with these environmental policies have been important in growing the AD industry, the discovery of value-added digestion products will ensure commercial viability long-term.

Rai et al. (2022) reported the GWP range for RNG production to be dependent on the pathway, ranging from -229 kg CO2e/GJ to 27 kg CO2e/GJ for waste feedstocks such as manure. Kolb et al. (2021) estimated the GWP for RNG production using energy crops as a feedstock to range from 10 kg CO2e/GJ to 88 kg CO2e/GJ. All our GWP values are lower than fossil natural gas, estimated at 53 kg CO2e/GJ (U.S. EPA, 2023a). Indirect environmental benefits such as the reduction of nutrient runoff and potential soil erosion improvements from biochar application could further reduce the GWP score of all scenarios (Ahmed et al., 2019; Matustík et al., 2020).

One limitation of our study is not considering the wide ecological benefits from an increase in prairie biomass cultivation (Hartman et al., 2011). Another limitation within our study is the assumption that a large-scale digester would produce equivalent biogas rates to a laboratory sized digester. This study also assumes the available N content within the biochar is enough to displace synthetic N fertilizer, as Ippolito et al. (2020) determined biochar should have at least 0.2 wt.% available N content to meet feasible and economic biochar application conditions.
57: <header>Discussion </header>

We investigated the feasibility of a pretreatment for prairie biomass to extract HCA co- products and reduce the recalcitrance of biomass for AD. Based on the MFSP, we determined the added costs for pretreatment and larger downstream units are justified by the increased revenue from higher RNG production and HCA generation. Within our study, HCA acts as a low-risk co- product due to utilization of substrate residue from the pretreatment process. Inclusion of diversified co-products within a system can also decrease the risks associated with fuels in addition to lowering the MFSP (Brandt et al., 2020). However, larger gas yields require larger downstream units.

According to switchgrass harvest rates from Kimura et al. (2015), about 1370 ha would be required to cultivate the biomass to feed our facility for one year. Based on the sensitivity analysis, a higher ratio of prairie biomass to manure could further decrease the MFSP. This may

further incentivize prairie biomass generation as some farmers have exhibited hesitation growing cellulosic feedstocks, due partially to concerns of profitability (Fewell et al., 2016). Additionally, there is a growing market for HCA, as Ranjan & Baghel (2023) predicts the Asia Pacific region to have a market value for HCA to be $86 million by 2031, a regional increase of 45% from 2021. HCA can be exploited for many applications within the pharmaceutical, beauty, food, and chemical industries for having admirable traits such as anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, and anti- aging properties (Flourat, et al., 2020; Ranjan & Baghel (2023). Products containing HCA can also earn natural labels while simultaneously promoting a circular economy relying on biomass cultivation (Flourat et al., 2020).

Nelissen et al. (2020) completed a literature review of RNG prices, stating current prices of RNG range $18/GJ to $46/GJ, while prices are projected to lower to $14/GJ to $20/GJ by 2050. Between 2014 and 2022, U.S. liquified natural gas export prices ranged from $5.2/GJ and $15.8/GJ (U.S. EIA 2023). Thus, the current scenarios would need public incentives to compete economically with natural gas. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Renewable Fuels Standard offers RIN credits valued at $1.43/gal of renewable fuel generated (U.S. EPA, 2023b). In 2023, cellulosic biofuel RINS have the highest value ranging from $2/RIN to $3/RIN ($25/GJ to $37/GJ).

The California Air Resources Board determined in just one year a typical livestock farm receives $865,000 from LCFS credits alone (Lee & Sumner, 2018). California's LCFS offers credits that range from $9.5/GJ to $42.6/GJ (California Air Resources Board, 2023b), depending on the technology's CI score. For a CI score of -7 g CO2e/MJ, the 2022 calculated credit is $9.15/GJ, while the calculated credit is $6.69/GJ for a 20 g CO2e/MJ score (California Air Resources Board, 2023b). The LCFS average credit price of 2022 was $121.1/t CO2e (California

Air Resources Board, 2023a). Thus, a combination of RINS and LCFS credit would make this technology economically competitive in the U.S. While the financial incentives associated with these environmental policies have been important in growing the AD industry, the discovery of value-added digestion products will ensure commercial viability long-term.

Rai et al. (2022) reported the GWP range for RNG production to be dependent on the pathway, ranging from -229 kg CO2e/GJ to 27 kg CO2e/GJ for waste feedstocks such as manure. Kolb et al. (2021) estimated the GWP for RNG production using energy crops as a feedstock to range from 10 kg CO2e/GJ to 88 kg CO2e/GJ. All our GWP values are lower than fossil natural gas, estimated at 53 kg CO2e/GJ (U.S. EPA, 2023a). Indirect environmental benefits such as the reduction of nutrient runoff and potential soil erosion improvements from biochar application could further reduce the GWP score of all scenarios (Ahmed et al., 2019; Matustík et al., 2020).

One limitation of our study is not considering the wide ecological benefits from an increase in prairie biomass cultivation (Hartman et al., 2011). Another limitation within our study is the assumption that a large-scale digester would produce equivalent biogas rates to a laboratory sized digester. This study also assumes the available N content within the biochar is enough to displace synthetic N fertilizer, as Ippolito et al. (2020) determined biochar should have at least 0.2 wt.% available N content to meet feasible and economic biochar application conditions.

62: <header>Conclusion </header>

This study has demonstrated that incorporation of prairie biomass pretreatment for co- digestion with manure for production of RNG, HCA, and biochar has a significant impact on both the economic feasibility and the environmental footprint of the process. All pretreatment scenarios showed smaller MFSPs compared to the Control, even with additional operating costs

from the pretreatment solution. This is due to the additional revenue stream (HCA) and increased digestibility of the biomass from the pretreatment process. The environmental impact from the pretreatment increased, primarily due to the use of ethanol. However, enhancement of the feedstock efficiency was positively impacted from this change.

Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses highlighted key parameters as crucial factors impacting both the economic and environmental outcomes of the scenarios, such as RNG yield and the prairie biomass to manure ratio. The substantial overlap in the emissions uncertainty between the pretreatment scenarios suggests that there may not be significant differences in terms of environmental impact when using biochar or varying the recycle rate. Given the current prices of natural gas, RNG from these scenarios would need public incentives such as those provided by California's LCFS or the U.S. RFS2 to be competitive. With these credits, however, these technological options for RNG production could be viable.
63: <header>Conclusion </header>

This study has demonstrated that incorporation of prairie biomass pretreatment for co- digestion with manure for production of RNG, HCA, and biochar has a significant impact on both the economic feasibility and the environmental footprint of the process. All pretreatment scenarios showed smaller MFSPs compared to the Control, even with additional operating costs

from the pretreatment solution. This is due to the additional revenue stream (HCA) and increased digestibility of the biomass from the pretreatment process. The environmental impact from the pretreatment increased, primarily due to the use of ethanol. However, enhancement of the feedstock efficiency was positively impacted from this change.

Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses highlighted key parameters as crucial factors impacting both the economic and environmental outcomes of the scenarios, such as RNG yield and the prairie biomass to manure ratio. The substantial overlap in the emissions uncertainty between the pretreatment scenarios suggests that there may not be significant differences in terms of environmental impact when using biochar or varying the recycle rate. Given the current prices of natural gas, RNG from these scenarios would need public incentives such as those provided by California's LCFS or the U.S. RFS2 to be competitive. With these credits, however, these technological options for RNG production could be viable.

[2] Complutense University of Madrid, Avda. Complutense S/N, 28040, Madrid, Spain</list>
1: <header>Abstract </header>

Renewable natural gas (RNG) from farm anaerobic digestion (AD) can provide clean energy and additional products to farmers. This study focuses on a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) for the co-digestion of cattle manure and prairie biomass utilizing a biogas model (ADM1). While the full synergistic relationship of cattle manure and prairie biomass was not entirely captured, the addition of manure to biomass increased methane (CH4) production for all scenarios analyzed. The scenario with a ratio of 1:9 volatile solids (VS) of prairie biomass to manure had the lowest estimated minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) at a decrease of 19.4% when compared to a ratio of 10:0. While the feedstock price for biomass was almost three times greater than that of cattle manure, the feedstock price did not significantly impact the MFSP due to the VS content in each feedstock. All impact categories analyzed had avoided burdens from the use of manure. Burdening effects from the cultivation of prairie grass were seen in almost all categories due to use of materials such as diesel and fertilizers, except ecotoxicity.
2: <header>Introduction </header>

Biogas from anaerobic digestion (AD) is becoming a widely explored energy source within the U.S. (U.S. EPA, 2023). Co-digestion, the addition of more than one substrate to a digester, is a common approach for optimizing feedstock digestibility (Hagos et al., 2017). Mono-digestion of manure can lead to process instability by supplying the reactor with a low

carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratio (Fernandes et al., 2009). The mono-digestion of lignocellulosic material is often infeasible due to the recalcitrant properties of the feedstock and high C/N ratios available (Karki et al, 2021; Paul & Dutta, 2018). Blending of manure and lignocellulosic material for co-digestion can provide optimal C/N ratios by balancing nutrient flows to the digester (Neshat, et al, 2017). Additional improvements of methane (CH4) yields from co- digestion could further be attributed to synergistic effects of mixing feedstocks, such as an increase in microbial variations and substrate interactions within the digester (Kunatsa & Xia, 2022; Ma et al., 2020).

Accurate biogas modeling tools may help relieve time and economic burdens of experimenters and increase economic and environmental assessments of AD. Several models representing AD processes have been created, including the Anaerobic Digestion Model No. 1 (ADM1), Gaussian, and Gompertz (Emebu et al., 2022). Modeling AD processes have become more complex throughout the years, now including steps like characterizing the feedstock by organic components, such as carbohydrates, to model the entire digestion process (Weinrich & Nelles, 2021). The International Water Association created the ADM1 in 2002 (Batstone et al, 2002). The ADM1 is a complex model including 24 dynamic variables, 19 biochemical conversion processes, and 8 algebraic variables. This model encompasses five steps to represent the AD process: disintegration, hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis, and methanogenesis. We implemented the ADM1 within Python to represent AD due to the accuracy and flexibility of this model.

The ADM1 model has been further expanded since 2002, including various methodologies for unique feedstock integration and improvement of nutrient interactions (Chen et al., 2016; Koch et al., 2010; Li et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2011). While the ADM1 is widely used

for biogas and CH4 estimation, there are still areas within the model that require further exploration, such as ammonia inhibition (Mo et al., 2023). There are very few economic and environmental focused studies utilizing the ADM1 found within literature. Glivin & Sekhar (2018) completed an economic analysis using experimental data and the ADM1 to determine costs of a digester within an institution in India. Usack et al. (2019) utilized the ADM1 within an environmental assessment for an AD combined heat and power system on a dairy farm.

This article describes a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) utilizing the ADM1 for the digestion of cattle manure and prairie biomass. Within this study, eleven ratios of manure and prairie biomass will be analyzed, including mono-digestion and co- digestion scenarios. Our work utilizes Wild et al. (In Preparation) for the process design of biogas upgrading to RNG, pyrolysis of the digestate, and the TEA methodology. An extended LCA uses the boundary expansion method for biochar inclusion. Additionally, we employ physical, economic, and energy allocation methodologies to determine environmental impacts of the products (RNG and biochar).
Materials and Methods
Integration of ADM1 for Manure and Prairie Grass Co-Digestion Modelling
Feedstock Characterization

Due to the complex and comprehensive nature of the ADM1, every employment of this model should include steps for accurate representation of the feedstocks (Klimiuk et al., 2015; Poggio et al, 2016). There are several different methodologies for substrate integration, including proper feedstock characterization, calculation of stochiometric parameters, and calibration of kinetic parameters (Koch et al., 2010; Xie et al., 2016; Zhou et al, 2011). Methodology for
feedstock characterization closely follows Koch et al. (2010), where feedstocks for the model are fractioned based on the organic matter within the material.

The feedstock's volatile solids (VS) are fractioned into proteins (Rp), lipids (RL), and carbohydrates (raw fiber and nitrogen-free extract (RF + NfE)). Neutral detergent fiber (NDF), acid detergent fiber (ADF), and acid detergent lignin (ADL) are used to further categorize the carbohydrate fraction as starch (RF +NfE-NDF), - cellulose (ADF - ADL), hemicellulose (NDF - ADF), and lignin (ADL). Figure 3.1 shows the composition breakdown. We obtained the composition for each feedstock from a literature review, averaging the values found for cattle manure and prairie biomass. The average of three prairie biomass species were used to categorize prairie biomass: switchgrass (Panicum virgatum L.), indiangrass (Sorghastrum Nutans L.), and big bluestem (Andropogon Gerardii). Table 3.1 summarizes the feedstocks.

After substrate characterization, NfE was calculated following Zhou et al. (2011), shown in Eq. 3-1.
Equation 3-1

As ADM1 quantifies the system with respect to the chemical oxygen demand (COD), the theoretical oxygen demand (ThOD) is used to quantify the degradability of proteins, lipids, starch, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. As lignin has no defined structure, Nadji et al. (2009) determined the formula from Stipa Tenacissima (L.). Lignin is entirely inert within this model while lipids, proteins, and starches are entirely biodegradable. Cellulose and hemicellulose are partially degradable. Table 3.2 displays the ThOD for each fraction within the model (Koch et al., 2010).
Table 3.2: Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD) of substrate fractions.
Substrate|Elemental Formula|ThOD (go2/gTs) Protein (ThODp)|C5H7O2N|1.42 Lipid (ThODL)|C57H104O6|2.90 Starch, cellulose, hemicellulose (ThODch)|(C6H10O5)n|1.19 Lignin (ThOD1)|C10.92H114.24O5.76|1.56

The composite material (Xc) for each feedstock was calculated via the summation of the ThOD for each fraction multiplied by the amount of the fraction present from the feedstock, shown in Eq. 3-2 (expressed in kgCOD/m³. The density (p) of each substrate inside the digester was assumed to be 1000 kg/m3.
Equation 3-2
c=(p)(TS)[Rp(ThODp) + (ThODL) + ADL(ThOD1) + (Rf+NfE-ADL)ThODch]

The public literature was used to determine the degradation percentages (Dvs) for each feedstock as 49.5% and 36.1% for cattle manure and prairie biomass, respectively (Jabloíski et al., 2015, Niu et al., 2015). We also integrated fixed stoichiometric parameters, f-factors, from Koch et al. (2010) for feedstock characterization. These f-factors (fch_xc, LL_xc, fP_xc, and fI_xc)

allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as|allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures|carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation|the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation coefficient, d (%), calculated from the determined degradability of the substrate (Dvs) and the|coefficient, d (%), calculated from the determined degradability of the substrate (Dvs) and the substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable|substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable parts of cellulose and|hemicellulose. Eq. 3-4,3-5,3-6, and 3-7 show the f-factor calculations, all represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD.|represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD. Equation 3-3|d==NDR-VS-A-Dus Equation 3-4| Equation 3-5|fLxc=R Equation 3-6| Equation 3-7|

Additional characterization is required for manure, as the substrate has already been partially disintegrated from the cow's rumen (Zhou et al., 2011). Soluble components for monosaccharides (Ssu), amino acids (Saa), long-chain fatty acids (Sfa), and inerts (Si) are

calculated based on the Xc, then subtracted from the individual components as Zhou et al., 2011 proposed. Ssu was subtracted from Xch, Saa was subtracted from Xpr, Sfa was subtracted from X1i, and Si was subtracted from Xi. Table 3.3 highlights the soluble percentages within manure from Wichern et al. (2008).
Table 3.3: Soluble components from disintegration of cattle manure. As acetate's solubility was determined to be 0.0 by Wichern et al. (2008), it was not considered in calculations.
Parameter|Indicator|Solubility (% COD) Ssu|Monosaccharides|10.8 Saa|Amino acids|2.7 Sfa|Total long chain acids|0.8 Si|Inerts|5.7

Finally, each feedstock was calibrated using trial and error based on the ADM1 calibration parameters available within the literature for similar feedstocks. Various calibration parameters were tested against experimental CH4 production rates found within the literature. All other parameters are based off the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (Rosén & Jeppsson, 2006).

As ADM1 quantifies the system with respect to the chemical oxygen demand (COD), the theoretical oxygen demand (ThOD) is used to quantify the degradability of proteins, lipids, starch, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as|allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures|carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation|the sum of all components equate to Xc. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable|substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable parts of cellulose and|hemicellulose. Eq. 3-4,3-5,3-6, and 3-7 show the f-factor calculations, all represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD.|represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD. Equation 3-3|d==NDR-VS-A-Dus Equation 3-4| Equation 3-5|fLxc=R Equation 3-6| Equation 3-7|

Additional characterization is required for manure, as the substrate has already been partially disintegrated from the cow's rumen (Zhou et al., 2011). Soluble components for monosaccharides (Ssu), amino acids (Saa), long-chain fatty acids (Sfa), and inerts (Si) are

calculated based on the Xc, then subtracted from the individual components as Zhou et al., 2011 proposed. Ssu was subtracted from Xch, Saa was subtracted from Xpr, Sfa was subtracted from X1i, and Si was subtracted from Xi. Table 3.3 highlights the soluble percentages within manure from Wichern et al. (2008).
Table 3.3: Soluble components from disintegration of cattle manure. As acetate's solubility was determined to be 0.0 by Wichern et al. (2008), it was not considered in calculations.
Parameter|Indicator|Solubility (% COD) Ssu|Monosaccharides|10.8 Saa|Amino acids|2.7 Sfa|Total long chain acids|0.8 Si|Inerts|5.7

Finally, each feedstock was calibrated using trial and error based on the ADM1 calibration parameters available within the literature for similar feedstocks. Various calibration parameters were tested against experimental CH4 production rates found within the literature. All other parameters are based off the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (Rosén & Jeppsson, 2006).
5: <header>Introduction </header>

Biogas from anaerobic digestion (AD) is becoming a widely explored energy source within the U.S. (U.S. EPA, 2023). Co-digestion, the addition of more than one substrate to a digester, is a common approach for optimizing feedstock digestibility (Hagos et al., 2017). Mono-digestion of manure can lead to process instability by supplying the reactor with a low

carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratio (Fernandes et al., 2009). The mono-digestion of lignocellulosic material is often infeasible due to the recalcitrant properties of the feedstock and high C/N ratios available (Karki et al, 2021; Paul & Dutta, 2018). Blending of manure and lignocellulosic material for co-digestion can provide optimal C/N ratios by balancing nutrient flows to the digester (Neshat, et al, 2017). Additional improvements of methane (CH4) yields from co- digestion could further be attributed to synergistic effects of mixing feedstocks, such as an increase in microbial variations and substrate interactions within the digester (Kunatsa & Xia, 2022; Ma et al., 2020).

Accurate biogas modeling tools may help relieve time and economic burdens of experimenters and increase economic and environmental assessments of AD. Several models representing AD processes have been created, including the Anaerobic Digestion Model No. 1 (ADM1), Gaussian, and Gompertz (Emebu et al., 2022). Modeling AD processes have become more complex throughout the years, now including steps like characterizing the feedstock by organic components, such as carbohydrates, to model the entire digestion process (Weinrich & Nelles, 2021). The International Water Association created the ADM1 in 2002 (Batstone et al, 2002). The ADM1 is a complex model including 24 dynamic variables, 19 biochemical conversion processes, and 8 algebraic variables. This model encompasses five steps to represent the AD process: disintegration, hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis, and methanogenesis. We implemented the ADM1 within Python to represent AD due to the accuracy and flexibility of this model.

The ADM1 model has been further expanded since 2002, including various methodologies for unique feedstock integration and improvement of nutrient interactions (Chen et al., 2016; Koch et al., 2010; Li et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2011). While the ADM1 is widely used

for biogas and CH4 estimation, there are still areas within the model that require further exploration, such as ammonia inhibition (Mo et al., 2023). There are very few economic and environmental focused studies utilizing the ADM1 found within literature. Glivin & Sekhar (2018) completed an economic analysis using experimental data and the ADM1 to determine costs of a digester within an institution in India. Usack et al. (2019) utilized the ADM1 within an environmental assessment for an AD combined heat and power system on a dairy farm.

This article describes a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) utilizing the ADM1 for the digestion of cattle manure and prairie biomass. Within this study, eleven ratios of manure and prairie biomass will be analyzed, including mono-digestion and co- digestion scenarios. Our work utilizes Wild et al. (In Preparation) for the process design of biogas upgrading to RNG, pyrolysis of the digestate, and the TEA methodology. An extended LCA uses the boundary expansion method for biochar inclusion. Additionally, we employ physical, economic, and energy allocation methodologies to determine environmental impacts of the products (RNG and biochar).
Materials and Methods
Integration of ADM1 for Manure and Prairie Grass Co-Digestion Modelling
Feedstock Characterization

Due to the complex and comprehensive nature of the ADM1, every employment of this model should include steps for accurate representation of the feedstocks (Klimiuk et al., 2015; Poggio et al, 2016). There are several different methodologies for substrate integration, including proper feedstock characterization, calculation of stochiometric parameters, and calibration of kinetic parameters (Koch et al., 2010; Xie et al., 2016; Zhou et al, 2011). Methodology for
feedstock characterization closely follows Koch et al. (2010), where feedstocks for the model are fractioned based on the organic matter within the material.

The feedstock's volatile solids (VS) are fractioned into proteins (Rp), lipids (RL), and carbohydrates (raw fiber and nitrogen-free extract (RF + NfE)). Neutral detergent fiber (NDF), acid detergent fiber (ADF), and acid detergent lignin (ADL) are used to further categorize the carbohydrate fraction as starch (RF +NfE-NDF), - cellulose (ADF - ADL), hemicellulose (NDF - ADF), and lignin (ADL). Figure 3.1 shows the composition breakdown. We obtained the composition for each feedstock from a literature review, averaging the values found for cattle manure and prairie biomass. The average of three prairie biomass species were used to categorize prairie biomass: switchgrass (Panicum virgatum L.), indiangrass (Sorghastrum Nutans L.), and big bluestem (Andropogon Gerardii). Table 3.1 summarizes the feedstocks.

The ADM1 model has been further expanded since 2002, including various methodologies for unique feedstock integration and improvement of nutrient interactions (Chen et al., 2016; Koch et al., 2010; Li et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2011). While the ADM1 is widely used

for biogas and CH4 estimation, there are still areas within the model that require further exploration, such as ammonia inhibition (Mo et al., 2023). There are very few economic and environmental focused studies utilizing the ADM1 found within literature. Glivin & Sekhar (2018) completed an economic analysis using experimental data and the ADM1 to determine costs of a digester within an institution in India. Usack et al. (2019) utilized the ADM1 within an environmental assessment for an AD combined heat and power system on a dairy farm.

This article describes a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) utilizing the ADM1 for the digestion of cattle manure and prairie biomass. Within this study, eleven ratios of manure and prairie biomass will be analyzed, including mono-digestion and co- digestion scenarios. Our work utilizes Wild et al. (In Preparation) for the process design of biogas upgrading to RNG, pyrolysis of the digestate, and the TEA methodology. An extended LCA uses the boundary expansion method for biochar inclusion. Additionally, we employ physical, economic, and energy allocation methodologies to determine environmental impacts of the products (RNG and biochar).
Integration of ADM1 for Manure and Prairie Grass Co-Digestion Modelling

Due to the complex and comprehensive nature of the ADM1, every employment of this model should include steps for accurate representation of the feedstocks (Klimiuk et al., 2015; Poggio et al, 2016). There are several different methodologies for substrate integration, including proper feedstock characterization, calculation of stochiometric parameters, and calibration of kinetic parameters (Koch et al., 2010; Xie et al., 2016; Zhou et al, 2011). Methodology for
feedstock characterization closely follows Koch et al. (2010), where feedstocks for the model are fractioned based on the organic matter within the material.

The feedstock's volatile solids (VS) are fractioned into proteins (Rp), lipids (RL), and carbohydrates (raw fiber and nitrogen-free extract (RF + NfE)). Neutral detergent fiber (NDF), acid detergent fiber (ADF), and acid detergent lignin (ADL) are used to further categorize the carbohydrate fraction as starch (RF +NfE-NDF), - cellulose (ADF - ADL), hemicellulose (NDF - ADF), and lignin (ADL). Figure 3.1 shows the composition breakdown. We obtained the composition for each feedstock from a literature review, averaging the values found for cattle manure and prairie biomass. The average of three prairie biomass species were used to categorize prairie biomass: switchgrass (Panicum virgatum L.), indiangrass (Sorghastrum Nutans L.), and big bluestem (Andropogon Gerardii). Table 3.1 summarizes the feedstocks.

As ADM1 quantifies the system with respect to the chemical oxygen demand (COD), the theoretical oxygen demand (ThOD) is used to quantify the degradability of proteins, lipids, starch, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. As lignin has no defined structure, Nadji et al. (2009) determined the formula from Stipa Tenacissima (L.). Lignin is entirely inert within this model while lipids, proteins, and starches are entirely biodegradable. Cellulose and hemicellulose are partially degradable. allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as|allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable parts of cellulose and|hemicellulose. Additional characterization is required for manure, as the substrate has already been partially disintegrated from the cow's rumen (Zhou et al., 2011). Soluble components for monosaccharides (Ssu), amino acids (Saa), long-chain fatty acids (Sfa), and inerts (Si) are

calculated based on the Xc, then subtracted from the individual components as Zhou et al., 2011 proposed. Finally, each feedstock was calibrated using trial and error based on the ADM1 calibration parameters available within the literature for similar feedstocks. Various calibration parameters were tested against experimental CH4 production rates found within the literature. All other parameters are based off the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (Rosén & Jeppsson, 2006).
8: Biogas from anaerobic digestion (AD) is becoming a widely explored energy source within the U.S. (U.S. EPA, 2023). Co-digestion, the addition of more than one substrate to a digester, is a common approach for optimizing feedstock digestibility (Hagos et al., 2017). Mono-digestion of manure can lead to process instability by supplying the reactor with a low

carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratio (Fernandes et al., 2009). The mono-digestion of lignocellulosic material is often infeasible due to the recalcitrant properties of the feedstock and high C/N ratios available (Karki et al, 2021; Paul & Dutta, 2018). Blending of manure and lignocellulosic material for co-digestion can provide optimal C/N ratios by balancing nutrient flows to the digester (Neshat, et al, 2017). Additional improvements of methane (CH4) yields from co- digestion could further be attributed to synergistic effects of mixing feedstocks, such as an increase in microbial variations and substrate interactions within the digester (Kunatsa & Xia, 2022; Ma et al., 2020).

Accurate biogas modeling tools may help relieve time and economic burdens of experimenters and increase economic and environmental assessments of AD. Modeling AD processes have become more complex throughout the years, now including steps like characterizing the feedstock by organic components, such as carbohydrates, to model the entire digestion process (Weinrich & Nelles, 2021). The International Water Association created the ADM1 in 2002 (Batstone et al, 2002). The ADM1 is a complex model including 24 dynamic variables, 19 biochemical conversion processes, and 8 algebraic variables. All other parameters are based off the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (Rosén & Jeppsson, 2006).
10: <header>Introduction </header>

Biogas from anaerobic digestion (AD) is becoming a widely explored energy source within the U.S. (U.S. EPA, 2023). Co-digestion, the addition of more than one substrate to a digester, is a common approach for optimizing feedstock digestibility (Hagos et al., 2017). Mono-digestion of manure can lead to process instability by supplying the reactor with a low

carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratio (Fernandes et al., 2009). The mono-digestion of lignocellulosic material is often infeasible due to the recalcitrant properties of the feedstock and high C/N ratios available (Karki et al, 2021; Paul & Dutta, 2018). Blending of manure and lignocellulosic material for co-digestion can provide optimal C/N ratios by balancing nutrient flows to the digester (Neshat, et al, 2017). Additional improvements of methane (CH4) yields from co- digestion could further be attributed to synergistic effects of mixing feedstocks, such as an increase in microbial variations and substrate interactions within the digester (Kunatsa & Xia, 2022; Ma et al., 2020).

Accurate biogas modeling tools may help relieve time and economic burdens of experimenters and increase economic and environmental assessments of AD. Several models representing AD processes have been created, including the Anaerobic Digestion Model No. 1 (ADM1), Gaussian, and Gompertz (Emebu et al., 2022). Modeling AD processes have become more complex throughout the years, now including steps like characterizing the feedstock by organic components, such as carbohydrates, to model the entire digestion process (Weinrich & Nelles, 2021). The International Water Association created the ADM1 in 2002 (Batstone et al, 2002). The ADM1 is a complex model including 24 dynamic variables, 19 biochemical conversion processes, and 8 algebraic variables. This model encompasses five steps to represent the AD process: disintegration, hydrolysis, acidogenesis, acetogenesis, and methanogenesis. We implemented the ADM1 within Python to represent AD due to the accuracy and flexibility of this model.

The ADM1 model has been further expanded since 2002, including various methodologies for unique feedstock integration and improvement of nutrient interactions (Chen et al., 2016; Koch et al., 2010; Li et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2011). While the ADM1 is widely used

for biogas and CH4 estimation, there are still areas within the model that require further exploration, such as ammonia inhibition (Mo et al., 2023). There are very few economic and environmental focused studies utilizing the ADM1 found within literature. Glivin & Sekhar (2018) completed an economic analysis using experimental data and the ADM1 to determine costs of a digester within an institution in India. Usack et al. (2019) utilized the ADM1 within an environmental assessment for an AD combined heat and power system on a dairy farm.

This article describes a techno-economic analysis (TEA) and life cycle assessment (LCA) utilizing the ADM1 for the digestion of cattle manure and prairie biomass. Within this study, eleven ratios of manure and prairie biomass will be analyzed, including mono-digestion and co- digestion scenarios. Our work utilizes Wild et al. (In Preparation) for the process design of biogas upgrading to RNG, pyrolysis of the digestate, and the TEA methodology. An extended LCA uses the boundary expansion method for biochar inclusion. Additionally, we employ physical, economic, and energy allocation methodologies to determine environmental impacts of the products (RNG and biochar).
Materials and Methods
Integration of ADM1 for Manure and Prairie Grass Co-Digestion Modelling
Feedstock Characterization

Due to the complex and comprehensive nature of the ADM1, every employment of this model should include steps for accurate representation of the feedstocks (Klimiuk et al., 2015; Poggio et al, 2016). There are several different methodologies for substrate integration, including proper feedstock characterization, calculation of stochiometric parameters, and calibration of kinetic parameters (Koch et al., 2010; Xie et al., 2016; Zhou et al, 2011). Methodology for
feedstock characterization closely follows Koch et al. (2010), where feedstocks for the model are fractioned based on the organic matter within the material.

The feedstock's volatile solids (VS) are fractioned into proteins (Rp), lipids (RL), and carbohydrates (raw fiber and nitrogen-free extract (RF + NfE)). Neutral detergent fiber (NDF), acid detergent fiber (ADF), and acid detergent lignin (ADL) are used to further categorize the carbohydrate fraction as starch (RF +NfE-NDF), - cellulose (ADF - ADL), hemicellulose (NDF - ADF), and lignin (ADL). Figure 3.1 shows the composition breakdown. We obtained the composition for each feedstock from a literature review, averaging the values found for cattle manure and prairie biomass. The average of three prairie biomass species were used to categorize prairie biomass: switchgrass (Panicum virgatum L.), indiangrass (Sorghastrum Nutans L.), and big bluestem (Andropogon Gerardii). Table 3.1 summarizes the feedstocks.

After substrate characterization, NfE was calculated following Zhou et al. (2011), shown in Eq. 3-1.
Equation 3-1

As ADM1 quantifies the system with respect to the chemical oxygen demand (COD), the theoretical oxygen demand (ThOD) is used to quantify the degradability of proteins, lipids, starch, cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. As lignin has no defined structure, Nadji et al. (2009) determined the formula from Stipa Tenacissima (L.). Lignin is entirely inert within this model while lipids, proteins, and starches are entirely biodegradable. Cellulose and hemicellulose are partially degradable. Table 3.2 displays the ThOD for each fraction within the model (Koch et al., 2010).
Table 3.2: Theoretical Oxygen Demand (ThOD) of substrate fractions.
Substrate|Elemental Formula|ThOD (go2/gTs) Protein (ThODp)|C5H7O2N|1.42 Lipid (ThODL)|C57H104O6|2.90 Starch, cellulose, hemicellulose (ThODch)|(C6H10O5)n|1.19 Lignin (ThOD1)|C10.92H114.24O5.76|1.56

The composite material (Xc) for each feedstock was calculated via the summation of the ThOD for each fraction multiplied by the amount of the fraction present from the feedstock, shown in Eq. 3-2 (expressed in kgCOD/m³. The density (p) of each substrate inside the digester was assumed to be 1000 kg/m3.
Equation 3-2
c=(p)(TS)[Rp(ThODp) + (ThODL) + ADL(ThOD1) + (Rf+NfE-ADL)ThODch]

The public literature was used to determine the degradation percentages (Dvs) for each feedstock as 49.5% and 36.1% for cattle manure and prairie biomass, respectively (Jabloíski et al., 2015, Niu et al., 2015). We also integrated fixed stoichiometric parameters, f-factors, from Koch et al. (2010) for feedstock characterization. These f-factors (fch_xc, LL_xc, fP_xc, and fI_xc)

allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as|allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures|carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation|the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation coefficient, d (%), calculated from the determined degradability of the substrate (Dvs) and the|coefficient, d (%), calculated from the determined degradability of the substrate (Dvs) and the substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable|substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable parts of cellulose and|hemicellulose. Eq. 3-4,3-5,3-6, and 3-7 show the f-factor calculations, all represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD.|represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD. Equation 3-3|d==NDR-VS-A-Dus Equation 3-4| Equation 3-5|fLxc=R Equation 3-6| Equation 3-7|

Additional characterization is required for manure, as the substrate has already been partially disintegrated from the cow's rumen (Zhou et al., 2011). Soluble components for monosaccharides (Ssu), amino acids (Saa), long-chain fatty acids (Sfa), and inerts (Si) are

calculated based on the Xc, then subtracted from the individual components as Zhou et al., 2011 proposed. Ssu was subtracted from Xch, Saa was subtracted from Xpr, Sfa was subtracted from X1i, and Si was subtracted from Xi. Table 3.3 highlights the soluble percentages within manure from Wichern et al. (2008).
Table 3.3: Soluble components from disintegration of cattle manure. As acetate's solubility was determined to be 0.0 by Wichern et al. (2008), it was not considered in calculations.
Parameter|Indicator|Solubility (% COD) Ssu|Monosaccharides|10.8 Saa|Amino acids|2.7 Sfa|Total long chain acids|0.8 Si|Inerts|5.7

Finally, each feedstock was calibrated using trial and error based on the ADM1 calibration parameters available within the literature for similar feedstocks. Various calibration parameters were tested against experimental CH4 production rates found within the literature. All other parameters are based off the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (Rosén & Jeppsson, 2006).

The composite material (Xc) for each feedstock was calculated via the summation of the ThOD for each fraction multiplied by the amount of the fraction present from the feedstock, shown in Eq. 3-2 (expressed in kgCOD/m³. The public literature was used to determine the degradation percentages (Dvs) for each feedstock as 49.5% and 36.1% for cattle manure and prairie biomass, respectively (Jabloíski et al., 2015, Niu et al., 2015). We also integrated fixed stoichiometric parameters, f-factors, from Koch et al. (2010) for feedstock characterization. These f-factors (fch_xc, LL_xc, fP_xc, and fI_xc)

allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as|allocate the various component fractions of Xc, following the disintegration step as carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures|carbohydrates (XCh,), lipids (XL), proteins (Xp), and inert material (X1). Utilizing f-factors ensures the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation|the sum of all components equate to Xc. Calculations for the f-factors utilize a degradation coefficient, d (%), calculated from the determined degradability of the substrate (Dvs) and the|coefficient, d (%), calculated from the determined degradability of the substrate (Dvs) and the substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable|substrate composition. The degradation coefficient, shown in Eq. 3-3, determines the degradable parts of cellulose and|hemicellulose. Eq. 3-4,3-5,3-6, and 3-7 show the f-factor calculations, all represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD.|represented in terms of kgCOD/kgCOD. Equation 3-3|d==NDR-VS-A-Dus Equation 3-4| Equation 3-5|fLxc=R Equation 3-6| Equation 3-7|

Additional characterization is required for manure, as the substrate has already been partially disintegrated from the cow's rumen (Zhou et al., 2011). Soluble components for monosaccharides (Ssu), amino acids (Saa), long-chain fatty acids (Sfa), and inerts (Si) are

calculated based on the Xc, then subtracted from the individual components as Zhou et al., 2011 proposed. Ssu was subtracted from Xch, Saa was subtracted from Xpr, Sfa was subtracted from X1i, and Si was subtracted from Xi. Table 3.3 highlights the soluble percentages within manure from Wichern et al. (2008).
Table 3.3: Soluble components from disintegration of cattle manure. As acetate's solubility was determined to be 0.0 by Wichern et al. (2008), it was not considered in calculations.
Parameter|Indicator|Solubility (% COD) Ssu|Monosaccharides|10.8 Saa|Amino acids|2.7 Sfa|Total long chain acids|0.8 Si|Inerts|5.7

Finally, each feedstock was calibrated using trial and error based on the ADM1 calibration parameters available within the literature for similar feedstocks. Various calibration parameters were tested against experimental CH4 production rates found within the literature. All other parameters are based off the benchmark simulation model no. 2 (Rosén & Jeppsson, 2006).
13: We utilized version 2.37.4 of BioSTEAM in Python 3.11 (Cortes-Peña; Python Software Foundation, 2022) for the system design and implementation of the ADM1. Wild et al. (In Preparation) was used as a basis for biogas upgrading to RNG from the co-digestion of prairie biomass and cattle manure without the pretreatment process. The feedstocks are digested with a hydraulic retention time of 30 days under mesophilic conditions. The digestate is thermally treated for eventual soil application. After biogas is collected, hydrogen sulfide is removed from the gas stream. On-site combustion is completed to meet electricity and heat requirements, burning 5% of the biogas stream and purchased natural gas as needed. Carbon dioxide (CO2) is
removed from the gas stream by an amine scrubber system with monoethanolamine (MEA). The full process diagram is shown in Figure 3.2.
Figure 3.2: Full process diagram of a commercial biorefinery for the co-digestion of prairie biomass and cattle manure.

There were 11 ratios examined, each scenario changing based on the VS ratio of prairie biomass (B) and cattle manure (M). The amount of material entering the digester makes up 1000 kgVS/hr for all scenarios. Scenario B10:M0 requires 100% of the VS makeup from biomass while B9:M1 requires 90% of the VS makeup from biomass and 10% from manure, and SO on. The digester recycled water makes up 10% TS within the reactor for each scenario.

15: The capital costs of the BioSTEAM process units have been modified following Wild et al. (In Preparation) for obtaining cost estimates of the digester system, biomass shredder, and gas compressor. Wild et al. (In Preparation) determined several operating costs to be sensitive parameters impacting the minimum fuel selling price (MFSP) of RNG, including the cost of the prairie biomass, cattle manure, biochar, and the internal rate of return (IRR). For further analysis, we are expanding upon those four parameters from an additional literature review.

Wild et al., (In Preparation) determined the cost of prairie biomass from the break-even farm gate prices for switchgrass, at $105/tonne. Krohn (2015) reported farmers selling switchgrass at a price of $120/tonne to be profitable and may have the potential to outcompete corn and soy on low quality land. Additionally, the price of manure is now based on application rates of liquid manure to a crop field, calculated to be $41/tonne (Olsen & Leibold, 2022). While biochar prices in literature range extensively, Thengane et al. (2021) estimated the product to have a minimum selling price of $150/tonne. Finally, Rajendran et al. (2019) utilized an IRR of 7% for a biogas upgrading plant receiving incentives. All streams with associated costs are shown in Table 3.4.
Table 3.4: Prices for all feedstocks, chemicals, or co-products within the system.
Streams|Price ($/tonne) Cattle manure|41 Prairie biomass|120 Natural gas|245 MEA|1080 Biochar|150

17: Following the ISO 14,040 and 14,044 LCA framework, the goal of this attributional LCA is to compare impacts from various co-digestion ratios of cattle manure and prairie biomass (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The system boundary expansion method is used to quantify effects from the offset of biochar for substitution of nitrogen (N) synthetic fertilizer, as biochar has several beneficial nutrient qualities (Ding et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022). Additionally, allocation methods are used to determine products (RNG and biochar) responsibility of environmental impacts for the mono-digestion scenarios (B10:M0 and B0:M10). Mass, energy,
and economic allocation methods are used. The boundary of our LCA is well-to-gate, where any physical streams crossing that boundary will be quantified as a feedstock, waste, or product.

We used EcoInvent V3.10 within OpenLCA V2.1 as our environmental impact factor database (Ecoinvent Database, OpenLCA Database). The Tool of the Reduction and Assessment of Chemical and other environmental Impacts (TRACI) was used for quantification of all streams flowing between the defined scope boundaries (Bare, 2011). TRACI was developed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency for conducing LCAs. We analyzed nine different impact categories within this system: one climate change impact category (global warming), three ecosystem quality impact categories (eutrophication, acidification, and ecotoxicity), three categories impacting human health (respiratory effects, carcinogenics, and non-carcinogenics), and two categories impacting both human health and ecosystem quality (ozone layer depletion and photochemical oxidation). Our functional unit was considered as 1 GJ of RNG.

A transportation distance of 50 km was considered for chemicals entering the plant. Transportation was not considered for cattle manure and biomass as they were both assumed to be located near-by the facility. Natural gas is transported to the facility within a pressurized pipeline. The biomass entering the plant was Miscanthus sinensis, the closest perennial available within OpenLCA. We are considering cattle manure as avoided waste. Synthetic N fertilizer was avoided on a 1:1 mass basis (Han et al., 2013). The total N content of biochar derived from cattle manure digestion is 1.71% of the total dry wt. % (Schouten et al., 2012). The total N content of biochar derived from the digestion of herbaceous biomass and agro-industrial residues is 1.78% of the total dry wt. % (Miliotti et al., 2020). Biochar N levels were determined from the feedstock ratios for each scenario. A system wide CH4 leak was considered for the plant at a 2% volume basis of RNG collected. The LCA inventory can be seen in Table 3.5.
Table 3.5: LCA impact factor inventory from EcoInvent for the boundary expansion method for inclusion of synthetic N fertilizer offset.

Impact Category Potential|Prairie Biomass|Cattle Manure|Avoidance of Synthetic N Fertilizer|MEA|Natural Gas|CH4 Leak Acidification (mol H+ eq./kg)|2.1x10-2|-6.2x10-|-1.8|4.7x10-1|9.3x10-2|0 Ecotoxicity (kg g 2,4-D eq./kg)|-6.4x10-1|-1.9x10-2|-4.6|1.1|9.0x10-2|2.5x10-7 Eutrophication (kg N/kg)|2.6x10-4|-1.9x10-5|-6.3x10-3|1.0x10-2|7.5x10-5|0 Global Warming (kg CO2 eq./kg)|5.9x10-2|-3.2x10-2|-9.7|3.4|4.9x10-1|1.4x101 1 Ozone Depletion (kg CFC-11 eq./kg)|5.7x10-9|-1.8x10-9|-4.9x10-7|1.4x10-7|2.4 x10-7|0 Photochemical Oxidation (NOx eq./kg)|3.2x10-4|-6.2x10-5|-1.8x10-2|5.4x10- -3|8.5x10|1.7x10-3 -3 Carcinogenics (kg benzene eq./kg)|1.5x10-4|-5.0x10-5|-1.1x10-2|4.3x10-2|8.3x10-4|0 Non-Carcinogenics (kg toluene eq./kg)|5.3x10-1|-2.6x10-1|-5.6x101|1.1x101|4.5|1.5x10 3 Respiratory Effects (kg PM2.5 eq./kg)|1.2x10-4|-2.9x10-5|-7.7x10-3|3.3x10-3|3.5x10-4|0

An additional analysis was completed for the allocation of emissions based on the mass, economic, and energy content of the system products: RNG and biochar. Total impacts of each mono-digestion scenario were allocated based on the metric used and production rates of products. The mass allocation metric is based on the total mass production rate. The economic

allocation metric is based on the assigned and calculated price (MFSP) for biochar and RNG, respectively. The energy content of biochar and RNG was determined to be 22 MJ/kg and 52.5 MJ/kg, respectively (Lee et al., 2020).

20: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>
Goal and Scope Definition

Following the ISO 14,040 and 14,044 LCA framework, the goal of this attributional LCA is to compare impacts from various co-digestion ratios of cattle manure and prairie biomass (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The system boundary expansion method is used to quantify effects from the offset of biochar for substitution of nitrogen (N) synthetic fertilizer, as biochar has several beneficial nutrient qualities (Ding et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022). Additionally, allocation methods are used to determine products (RNG and biochar) responsibility of environmental impacts for the mono-digestion scenarios (B10:M0 and B0:M10). Mass, energy,
and economic allocation methods are used. The boundary of our LCA is well-to-gate, where any physical streams crossing that boundary will be quantified as a feedstock, waste, or product.

We used EcoInvent V3.10 within OpenLCA V2.1 as our environmental impact factor database (Ecoinvent Database, OpenLCA Database). The Tool of the Reduction and Assessment of Chemical and other environmental Impacts (TRACI) was used for quantification of all streams flowing between the defined scope boundaries (Bare, 2011). TRACI was developed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency for conducing LCAs. We analyzed nine different impact categories within this system: one climate change impact category (global warming), three ecosystem quality impact categories (eutrophication, acidification, and ecotoxicity), three categories impacting human health (respiratory effects, carcinogenics, and non-carcinogenics), and two categories impacting both human health and ecosystem quality (ozone layer depletion and photochemical oxidation). Our functional unit was considered as 1 GJ of RNG.

A transportation distance of 50 km was considered for chemicals entering the plant. Transportation was not considered for cattle manure and biomass as they were both assumed to be located near-by the facility. Natural gas is transported to the facility within a pressurized pipeline. The biomass entering the plant was Miscanthus sinensis, the closest perennial available within OpenLCA. We are considering cattle manure as avoided waste. Synthetic N fertilizer was avoided on a 1:1 mass basis (Han et al., 2013). The total N content of biochar derived from cattle manure digestion is 1.71% of the total dry wt. % (Schouten et al., 2012). The total N content of biochar derived from the digestion of herbaceous biomass and agro-industrial residues is 1.78% of the total dry wt. % (Miliotti et al., 2020). Biochar N levels were determined from the feedstock ratios for each scenario. A system wide CH4 leak was considered for the plant at a 2% volume basis of RNG collected. The LCA inventory can be seen in Table 3.5.
Table 3.5: LCA impact factor inventory from EcoInvent for the boundary expansion method for inclusion of synthetic N fertilizer offset.

21: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>
Goal and Scope Definition

Following the ISO 14,040 and 14,044 LCA framework, the goal of this attributional LCA is to compare impacts from various co-digestion ratios of cattle manure and prairie biomass (ISO, 2006a; ISO, 2006b). The system boundary expansion method is used to quantify effects from the offset of biochar for substitution of nitrogen (N) synthetic fertilizer, as biochar has several beneficial nutrient qualities (Ding et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2022). Additionally, allocation methods are used to determine products (RNG and biochar) responsibility of environmental impacts for the mono-digestion scenarios (B10:M0 and B0:M10). Mass, energy,
and economic allocation methods are used. The boundary of our LCA is well-to-gate, where any physical streams crossing that boundary will be quantified as a feedstock, waste, or product.

We used EcoInvent V3.10 within OpenLCA V2.1 as our environmental impact factor database (Ecoinvent Database, OpenLCA Database). The Tool of the Reduction and Assessment of Chemical and other environmental Impacts (TRACI) was used for quantification of all streams flowing between the defined scope boundaries (Bare, 2011). TRACI was developed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency for conducing LCAs. We analyzed nine different impact categories within this system: one climate change impact category (global warming), three ecosystem quality impact categories (eutrophication, acidification, and ecotoxicity), three categories impacting human health (respiratory effects, carcinogenics, and non-carcinogenics), and two categories impacting both human health and ecosystem quality (ozone layer depletion and photochemical oxidation). Our functional unit was considered as 1 GJ of RNG.

A transportation distance of 50 km was considered for chemicals entering the plant. Transportation was not considered for cattle manure and biomass as they were both assumed to be located near-by the facility. Natural gas is transported to the facility within a pressurized pipeline. The biomass entering the plant was Miscanthus sinensis, the closest perennial available within OpenLCA. We are considering cattle manure as avoided waste. Synthetic N fertilizer was avoided on a 1:1 mass basis (Han et al., 2013). The total N content of biochar derived from cattle manure digestion is 1.71% of the total dry wt. % (Schouten et al., 2012). The total N content of biochar derived from the digestion of herbaceous biomass and agro-industrial residues is 1.78% of the total dry wt. % (Miliotti et al., 2020). Biochar N levels were determined from the feedstock ratios for each scenario. A system wide CH4 leak was considered for the plant at a 2% volume basis of RNG collected. The LCA inventory can be seen in Table 3.5.
Table 3.5: LCA impact factor inventory from EcoInvent for the boundary expansion method for inclusion of synthetic N fertilizer offset.

An additional analysis was completed for the allocation of emissions based on the mass, economic, and energy content of the system products: RNG and biochar. Total impacts of each mono-digestion scenario were allocated based on the metric used and production rates of products. The mass allocation metric is based on the total mass production rate. The economic

allocation metric is based on the assigned and calculated price (MFSP) for biochar and RNG, respectively. The energy content of biochar and RNG was determined to be 22 MJ/kg and 52.5 MJ/kg, respectively (Lee et al., 2020).
22: <header>Simulated System Results </header>

The inlet concentration, Xc, was calculated for each feedstock before digestion, equaling 166 kgCOD/m³ and 1136 kgCOD/m³ for manure and prairie biomass, respectively. The degradation coefficient, d, used for determining the degradability of cellulose and hemicellulose, was larger for all co-digestion scenarios when compared to the mono-digestion scenarios. Additionally, f-factors fCh_xc and fp_xc linearly increased with the addition of manure while fI_xc and fL_xc linearly increased with the addition of biomass. Additional calculated and calibration parameters used are presented in the Appendix.

The mono-digestion of prairie biomass produced 156 mL/gVS of CH4 while the mono- digestion of manure produced 219 mL/gVS of CH4. All co-digestion scenarios had larger CH4 production rates than the mono-digestion of prairie biomass. Two out of the nine co-digestion scenarios produced larger CH4 rates than the mono-digestion of manure (B8:M2 and B9:M1), with the highest CH4 production rate at 227 mL/gVS (B9:M1). The relationship between the biogas yields and the CH4 yields correspond, varying from 293 mL/gVS to 427 mL/gVS (B10:M0 and B1:M9). The volumetric CH4 makeup of the gas ranged from 53% to 54%. The total CH4 and biogas produced from the plant is shown in Figure 3.3.

As the model is based on the VS content of the feedstocks, manure is required in a much greater quantity than biomass, with 6560 kg/hr of manure required for B0:M10 and 1210 kg/hr of biomass required for B10:M0. Digestate production levels increased with the addition of biomass, with the largest production rate of 557 kg/dry tonne (B10:M0). B1:M9 has the largest CH4 production from biogas while B0:M10 has the largest residual CH4 collected from the digestate. The residual CH4 from the digestate increases linearly with the addition of manure, from 57 m3/dry tonne to 139 m³ dry tonne. B1:M9 is the largest producer of RNG at 10.1 GJ/dry tonne. The RNG produced in B1:M9 is 39% larger than the RNG produced in B10:M0. Further results are displayed in Table 3.6.
Table 3.6: Production rates for RNG and biochar for all scenarios. Refer to materials and methods for scenario definition.

26: <header>Techno-Economic Cycle Analysis </header>

The estimated MFSP for all scenarios ranged from $41.88/GJ to $51.97/GJ, as shown in Figure 3.4. The MFSP is heavily dependent on the production of CH4 from digestion, as B1:M9 has the smallest estimated MFSP from an increase in feedstock efficiency and residual CH4 from the digestate. The difference in feedstock pricing does not have a large effect on the MFSP. This is due to difference of the VS content, as the biomass has a more digestible material on a mass basis. This results in a slightly larger cost for manure acquisition, as the total feedstock costs for B10:M0 and B0:M10 are $19.38/GJ and $20.60/GJ, respectively.

Figure 3.4: MFSP for all scenarios. Additional losses within the system make up the 'Materials' category. Refer to materials and methods for scenario definition.

The MFSP offset from the biochar increases with the addition of biomass and larger N content found within the biochar. This results in an offset of $5.91/GJ and $12.27/GJ for scenarios B0:M10 and B10:M0, respectively. Operating and maintenance (O&M) costs make up between 20% and 28% of the total MFSP while the return on investment (ROI) costs range from 21% to 27%. As the amine used for CO2 removal (MEA) is recycled, it does not have a large effect on the MFSP ($1.21/GJ to $1.25/GJ).

Capital costs within the system ranged from $13.31 million (B10:M0) to $13.84 million (B1:M9), with significant costs coming from the stripper column, boiler, and digester. This relationship closely mirrors the biogas production rates, as a larger biogas and CH4 volume requires larger downstream units for upgrading. For example, the absorber column equipment cost increased by 37% for B1:M9 when compared to B10:M0. All scenarios within this plant had a net present value (NPV) to reach a breakeven point. B0:M10 had the largest annual variable
operating cost due to a larger amount of feedstock required to meet VS feed conditions. B1:M9 had the largest calculated value of annual operating costs subtracted from the yearly sales of products.

30: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>

The LCA boundary of our AD upgrading facility was expanded to include impacts of biochar for the avoidance of synthetic N fertilizer. The total impact of the system is largely dependent on what feedstocks are being utilized, as manure has avoided burdens in all categories from being considered an avoided waste. All avoided burdens are represented with negative values while burdens impacting the system are represented with positive values. The global warming potential from the feedstocks alone ranged from -17.3 kg CO2 eq./GJ to 10.4 kg CO2 eq./GJ for B0:M10 and B10:M0, respectively. Biomass integration had a large estimated avoided burden on ecotoxicity potential, with the estimated net ecotoxicity ranging from -119 kg 2,4-D eq./GJ for B10:M0 (as compared with - -12 2,4-D eq./GJ for B0:M10). Ecotoxicity is the only category with avoided burdens associated with the integration of biomass. Figure 3.5 shows the results of the nine impact categories for the scenario with the smallest estimated MFSP (B1:M9).

N Fertilizer Avoided

Figure 3.5: Percentage breakdown of LCA results with each category summing to 100% for B1:M9. Refer to materials and methods for scenario definition.

The CH4 leak within the system did not vary largely, with the global warming potential estimated at 2.91 kg CO2 eq./GJ to 2.92 kg CO2 eq./GJ. The largest burdening contributor to the eutrophication impact category is MEA for scenarios B4:M6 through B0:M10, otherwise the largest contributor is biomass cultivation. MEA is also the largest contributor to the carcinogenics potential for all scenarios. Natural gas was the largest burdening contributor to ozone depletion category, with the largest estimation at 1.14x10-6 kg CFC-11 eq./GJ for B10:M0. Biochar has a significant influence on all categories from the avoided synthetic N fertilizer. Further results can be seen in Table 3.7.

We also considered an attributional LCA based on the mass, energy, and economics of each product. Table 3.8 displays the percentage breakdown used for assigning impacts to RNG and biochar for both mono-digestion scenarios to see the range of possible outcomes. Biochar received the majority of impacts for mass allocation due to the product's physical properties (69% to 83%). Energy allocation places more impact on the RNG due to the higher energy content of the RNG (34% to 51%). Economic allocation places the majority of the impacts on RNG due to large price difference of the products (80% to 87%). Scenarios with a larger use of manure places slightly more impact on the RNG for all allocation methods as there is a greater production of RNG (seen through comparing B10:M0 and B0:M10).
Table 3.8: Percentage allocation of environmental and human impact categories for biochar and RNG. Refer to materials and methods for scenario definition.

Scenario|B10:M0|B0:M10 Mass Allocation: Biochar|82.5%|69.4% Mass Allocation: RNG|17.5%|30.6% Energy Allocation: Biochar|66.5%|48.9% Energy Allocation: RNG|33.5%|51.1% Economic Allocation: Biochar|20.6%|13.2% Economic Allocation: RNG|79.7%|86.9%

The total global warming potential for B10:M0 was estimated at 19.81 kg CO2 eq./GJ without considering the impacts of biochar. Utilizing the above allocation emissions, RNG is responsible for 3.47 kg CO2 eq./GJ, 6.64 kg CO2 eq./GJ, or 15.8 kg CO2 eq./GJ dependent on the allocation method used.
31: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>

The LCA boundary of our AD upgrading facility was expanded to include impacts of biochar for the avoidance of synthetic N fertilizer. The total impact of the system is largely dependent on what feedstocks are being utilized, as manure has avoided burdens in all categories from being considered an avoided waste. All avoided burdens are represented with negative values while burdens impacting the system are represented with positive values. The global warming potential from the feedstocks alone ranged from -17.3 kg CO2 eq./GJ to 10.4 kg CO2 eq./GJ for B0:M10 and B10:M0, respectively. Biomass integration had a large estimated avoided burden on ecotoxicity potential, with the estimated net ecotoxicity ranging from -119 kg 2,4-D eq./GJ for B10:M0 (as compared with - -12 2,4-D eq./GJ for B0:M10). Ecotoxicity is the only category with avoided burdens associated with the integration of biomass. Figure 3.5 shows the results of the nine impact categories for the scenario with the smallest estimated MFSP (B1:M9).
Manure
Biomass
MEA
CH4 Leak
N Fertilizer Avoided
Natural Gas
Figure 3.5: Percentage breakdown of LCA results with each category summing to 100% for B1:M9. Refer to materials and methods for scenario definition.

The CH4 leak within the system did not vary largely, with the global warming potential estimated at 2.91 kg CO2 eq./GJ to 2.92 kg CO2 eq./GJ. The largest burdening contributor to the eutrophication impact category is MEA for scenarios B4:M6 through B0:M10, otherwise the largest contributor is biomass cultivation. MEA is also the largest contributor to the carcinogenics potential for all scenarios. Natural gas was the largest burdening contributor to ozone depletion category, with the largest estimation at 1.14x10-6 kg CFC-11 eq./GJ for B10:M0. Biochar has a significant influence on all categories from the avoided synthetic N fertilizer. Further results can be seen in Table 3.7.
