We also considered an attributional LCA based on the mass, energy, and economics of each product. Table 3.8 displays the percentage breakdown used for assigning impacts to RNG and biochar for both mono-digestion scenarios to see the range of possible outcomes. Biochar received the majority of impacts for mass allocation due to the product's physical properties (69% to 83%). Energy allocation places more impact on the RNG due to the higher energy content of the RNG (34% to 51%). Economic allocation places the majority of the impacts on RNG due to large price difference of the products (80% to 87%). Scenarios with a larger use of manure places slightly more impact on the RNG for all allocation methods as there is a greater production of RNG (seen through comparing B10:M0 and B0:M10).
Table 3.8: Percentage allocation of environmental and human impact categories for biochar and RNG. Refer to materials and methods for scenario definition.

Scenario|B10:M0|B0:M10 Mass Allocation: Biochar|82.5%|69.4% Mass Allocation: RNG|17.5%|30.6% Energy Allocation: Biochar|66.5%|48.9% Energy Allocation: RNG|33.5%|51.1% Economic Allocation: Biochar|20.6%|13.2% Economic Allocation: RNG|79.7%|86.9%

The total global warming potential for B10:M0 was estimated at 19.81 kg CO2 eq./GJ without considering the impacts of biochar. Utilizing the above allocation emissions, RNG is responsible for 3.47 kg CO2 eq./GJ, 6.64 kg CO2 eq./GJ, or 15.8 kg CO2 eq./GJ dependent on the allocation method used.
35: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>

The LCA boundary of our AD upgrading facility was expanded to include impacts of biochar for the avoidance of synthetic N fertilizer. The total impact of the system is largely dependent on what feedstocks are being utilized, as manure has avoided burdens in all categories from being considered an avoided waste. All avoided burdens are represented with negative values while burdens impacting the system are represented with positive values. The global warming potential from the feedstocks alone ranged from -17.3 kg CO2 eq./GJ to 10.4 kg CO2 eq./GJ for B0:M10 and B10:M0, respectively. Biomass integration had a large estimated avoided burden on ecotoxicity potential, with the estimated net ecotoxicity ranging from -119 kg 2,4-D eq./GJ for B10:M0 (as compared with - -12 2,4-D eq./GJ for B0:M10). Ecotoxicity is the only category with avoided burdens associated with the integration of biomass. Figure 3.5 shows the results of the nine impact categories for the scenario with the smallest estimated MFSP (B1:M9).

The CH4 leak within the system did not vary largely, with the global warming potential estimated at 2.91 kg CO2 eq./GJ to 2.92 kg CO2 eq./GJ. The largest burdening contributor to the eutrophication impact category is MEA for scenarios B4:M6 through B0:M10, otherwise the largest contributor is biomass cultivation. MEA is also the largest contributor to the carcinogenics potential for all scenarios. Natural gas was the largest burdening contributor to ozone depletion category, with the largest estimation at 1.14x10-6 kg CFC-11 eq./GJ for B10:M0. 37: The CH4 outputs from the ADM1 model for both mono-digestion scenarios fit into the reviewed literature. Reviewed CH4 yields from the mono-digestion of cattle manure ranged from 181 mL/gVS to 270 mL/gVS (Kafle & Chen, 2016; Liu & Sun, 2015; Moset et al., 2017; Tsapekos et al., 2018; Varol & Ugurlu, 2017). The CH4 yields for the mono-digestion of switchgrass have a larger range within literature, between 104 and 309 mL/gVS (Frigon et al.,

2012; Labatut et al., 2011; Massé et al., 2010; Zheng et al., 2015). Massé et al. (2010) theorized this trend is not due to the energy content of the biomass changing, but instead digestible material becoming more difficult to access from an increase in non-digestible material.

During a review of various feedstocks under co-digestion, Ma et al., 2020 determined the mean of CH4 produced via co-digested cattle manure increased by 38%. Zheng et al. (2015) and Awais et al. (2016) reported a CH4 increase from the co-digestion of manure and switchgrass without pretreatment ranged from 25% to 39%. As our largest CH4 increase from co-digestion was less than 6%, our model could be enhanced to capture this synergistic relationship more accurately. This relationship may be better represented if feedstock characterization and calibration was based on experimentation rather than literature. Additionally, integration of gas curves for estimation of the degradation coefficient (d) may represent the co-digestion scenarios more accurately, instead of calculating d. Our biogas prediction model also had a large amount of CO2 present within the biogas than is typically seen, as the CH4 content of the biogas is typically around 55% to 80% (Lusk, 1998).

The estimated MFSPs are largely dependent on the gas produced as there is an inverse relationship seen between the gas production rates and MFSPs. The estimated MFSP of co- digestion scenarios may be reduced further if the synergistic relationship was entirely captured within our model. The difference in feedstock price does not have a large impact on the MFSP as more manure is required to reach the VS digester feed. As biochar production increases with larger amounts of biomass utilized, the MFSP further decreases. If the biochar costs were set higher, this offset would be more significant and could favor scenarios with a larger biomass ratio.

Hegde & Trabold (2019) reported an integration of the co-digestion of animal manure to not only increase economic efficiency of a commercial scale AD, but also increase system stability. Efficient use of feedstocks and co-digestion implementation can decrease MFSP of RNG. While the biogas production requires higher capital costs, the additional sales revenue from an increase in RNG produced leads to a lower MFSP. Additionally, with our high CO2 makeup in the biogas from the ADM1 model, our estimation of downstream capital costs may be larger than they would be in reality.

The environmental assessments show a large difference for all impact categories analyzed based on the feedstock used. This is due to manure being an avoided waste, as avoided manure decreases the amount of CH4, ammonia, and nitrous oxide emitted to the atmosphere (Havukainen et al., 2020; Sajeev et al., 2018). The impacts seen from biomass cultivation can be attributed to use of resources such as diesel and fertilizers, which can result in ecosystem eutrophication, ozone depletion, and acidification (Larson, 2006; Zah et al., 2007). Additionally, the use of diesel for farm application can also result in non-carcinogenics, ecotoxicity, and acidification impacts (Ashworth et al., 2015).

Cultivation of perennial biomass crops can have positive effects on the land, apparent with the beneficial impacts within the ecotoxicity category. Perennials established on marginal land can lead to soil improvements such as a decrease in soil erosion and an increase in the amount of atmospheric CO2 captured (Fernando et al., 2018; Guzman et al., 2014; Voigt et al., 2012). Not all biomass requires the same amount of system inputs, as the cultivation of prairie biomass can require lower levels of fertilizer when compared to traditional crops, due partially to efficient absorption within the root system (Cadoux et al., 2012; Hill, 2009). For example,
switchgrass was reported to require less diesel fuel, pesticides, herbicides, and phosphorus nutrients than traditional row crops (Ashworth et al., 2015).

Allocation methods are typically based on the physical properties of the products themselves, such as energy or mass. Physical allocation is considered to be the most scientifically sound method of allocation methodology, as it is based on measurable properties of each product, capturing the relationships of the system (Timonen et al., 2019). Economic allocation, dependent on the product's price, has received criticism about being an unreliable method for impact assignment as it is dependent on the product's market and human preferences (Wilson et al., 2021). Allocation methods also typically do not consider the use of the product, such as nutrient capacity of biochar.

Each scenario's estimated global warming potential falls far below the global warming potential for diesel, estimated at 86 kg CO2 eq./GJ (Department for Transport, 2008). The utilization of RNG as a transportation fuel avoids the production and combustion of traditional diesel on roads (Ardolino et al., 2018). Additionally, RNG as a transportation fuel can improve the local air quality by reducing the of particulate matter and nitrogen oxides emitted, when compared to traditional fuel (U.S. EPA, 2024). Patterson et al. (2013) reported the optimization of an RNG system can further reduce CO2 emissions. This relationship may further be seen in our co-digestion LCA results if the ADM1 had further captured the synergistic relationship between cattle manure and prairie biomass.

39: <header>Discussion </header>

The CH4 outputs from the ADM1 model for both mono-digestion scenarios fit into the reviewed literature. Reviewed CH4 yields from the mono-digestion of cattle manure ranged from 181 mL/gVS to 270 mL/gVS (Kafle & Chen, 2016; Liu & Sun, 2015; Moset et al., 2017; Tsapekos et al., 2018; Varol & Ugurlu, 2017). The CH4 yields for the mono-digestion of switchgrass have a larger range within literature, between 104 and 309 mL/gVS (Frigon et al.,

2012; Labatut et al., 2011; Massé et al., 2010; Zheng et al., 2015). Massé et al. (2010) theorized this trend is not due to the energy content of the biomass changing, but instead digestible material becoming more difficult to access from an increase in non-digestible material.

During a review of various feedstocks under co-digestion, Ma et al., 2020 determined the mean of CH4 produced via co-digested cattle manure increased by 38%. Zheng et al. (2015) and Awais et al. (2016) reported a CH4 increase from the co-digestion of manure and switchgrass without pretreatment ranged from 25% to 39%. As our largest CH4 increase from co-digestion was less than 6%, our model could be enhanced to capture this synergistic relationship more accurately. This relationship may be better represented if feedstock characterization and calibration was based on experimentation rather than literature. Additionally, integration of gas curves for estimation of the degradation coefficient (d) may represent the co-digestion scenarios more accurately, instead of calculating d. Our biogas prediction model also had a large amount of CO2 present within the biogas than is typically seen, as the CH4 content of the biogas is typically around 55% to 80% (Lusk, 1998).

The estimated MFSPs are largely dependent on the gas produced as there is an inverse relationship seen between the gas production rates and MFSPs. The estimated MFSP of co- digestion scenarios may be reduced further if the synergistic relationship was entirely captured within our model. The difference in feedstock price does not have a large impact on the MFSP as more manure is required to reach the VS digester feed. As biochar production increases with larger amounts of biomass utilized, the MFSP further decreases. If the biochar costs were set higher, this offset would be more significant and could favor scenarios with a larger biomass ratio.

Hegde & Trabold (2019) reported an integration of the co-digestion of animal manure to not only increase economic efficiency of a commercial scale AD, but also increase system stability. Efficient use of feedstocks and co-digestion implementation can decrease MFSP of RNG. While the biogas production requires higher capital costs, the additional sales revenue from an increase in RNG produced leads to a lower MFSP. Additionally, with our high CO2 makeup in the biogas from the ADM1 model, our estimation of downstream capital costs may be larger than they would be in reality.

The environmental assessments show a large difference for all impact categories analyzed based on the feedstock used. This is due to manure being an avoided waste, as avoided manure decreases the amount of CH4, ammonia, and nitrous oxide emitted to the atmosphere (Havukainen et al., 2020; Sajeev et al., 2018). The impacts seen from biomass cultivation can be attributed to use of resources such as diesel and fertilizers, which can result in ecosystem eutrophication, ozone depletion, and acidification (Larson, 2006; Zah et al., 2007). Additionally, the use of diesel for farm application can also result in non-carcinogenics, ecotoxicity, and acidification impacts (Ashworth et al., 2015).

Cultivation of perennial biomass crops can have positive effects on the land, apparent with the beneficial impacts within the ecotoxicity category. Perennials established on marginal land can lead to soil improvements such as a decrease in soil erosion and an increase in the amount of atmospheric CO2 captured (Fernando et al., 2018; Guzman et al., 2014; Voigt et al., 2012). Not all biomass requires the same amount of system inputs, as the cultivation of prairie biomass can require lower levels of fertilizer when compared to traditional crops, due partially to efficient absorption within the root system (Cadoux et al., 2012; Hill, 2009). For example,
switchgrass was reported to require less diesel fuel, pesticides, herbicides, and phosphorus nutrients than traditional row crops (Ashworth et al., 2015).

Allocation methods are typically based on the physical properties of the products themselves, such as energy or mass. Physical allocation is considered to be the most scientifically sound method of allocation methodology, as it is based on measurable properties of each product, capturing the relationships of the system (Timonen et al., 2019). Economic allocation, dependent on the product's price, has received criticism about being an unreliable method for impact assignment as it is dependent on the product's market and human preferences (Wilson et al., 2021). Allocation methods also typically do not consider the use of the product, such as nutrient capacity of biochar.

Each scenario's estimated global warming potential falls far below the global warming potential for diesel, estimated at 86 kg CO2 eq./GJ (Department for Transport, 2008). The utilization of RNG as a transportation fuel avoids the production and combustion of traditional diesel on roads (Ardolino et al., 2018). Additionally, RNG as a transportation fuel can improve the local air quality by reducing the of particulate matter and nitrogen oxides emitted, when compared to traditional fuel (U.S. EPA, 2024). Patterson et al. (2013) reported the optimization of an RNG system can further reduce CO2 emissions. This relationship may further be seen in our co-digestion LCA results if the ADM1 had further captured the synergistic relationship between cattle manure and prairie biomass.

44: The addition of co-digestion within a system can create better nutrient levels, therefore increasing AD efficiency and higher gas production levels. As seen with B1:M9, this efficiency leads to smaller MFSPs. As our model did not accurately capture the synergistic relationship

between manure and prairie biomass, we expected to see this relationship for more co-digestion scenarios at a larger level. Further analysis should be completed on our ADM1 model implementation with additional integration from laboratory analysis. This may help to estimate TEA and LCA parameters more accurately for the co-digestion scenarios.

While biomass cultivated for co-digestion can increase environmental and health burdens due to use of resources, prairie biomass can require less materials for cultivation than traditional crops. These types of biomass could make an ideal energy crop for co-digestion if the inclusion would increase the efficiency and CH4 production of the system. Establishing prairie biomass can improve ecotoxicity levels by influencing the local environment positively, such as the reduction of soil eroded. RNG produced from the co-digestion of manure and prairie grass can increase CH4 levels, avoid environmental and health impacts from manure, and further improve ecosystem health from prairie biomass cultivation.

<title>CHAPTER 4. GENERAL CONCLUSION </title>

Improvements of AD systems from employment of pretreatment and inclusion of co- digestion processes can dramatically improve plant productivity. While pretreatment solutions and additional feedstocks can increase the operational costs, the increase in the CH4 yield justifies these investments with a net decrease of the MFSP. However, this volumetric gas increase requires larger downstream units, increasing the equipment costs of the plant significantly. Analysis of these plants confirms the efficient use of feedstocks has beneficial effects on the TEA results.

Environmental impacts from RNG are significantly lower than traditional fossil fuel counterparts, even with the addition of a chemical pretreatment and prairie biomass cultivation. Prairie biomass cultivation can have beneficial impacts on the environment while increasing the CH4 yields of our system from co-digestion synergies. Treatment of manure with AD also has several environmental and human health related benefits, such as lowering the amount of GHGs emitted to the atmosphere.

The process of upgrading biogas from AD technology can offer a unique energy product that provides revenue from upgrading residues and energy crops. RNG is a potential pathway for the avoidance of carbon emissions within the U.S. If provided with infrastructure to make RNG upgrading more profitable, such as LCFS and RIN credits, there could be further adoption of RNG biorefineries, creating better fuel alternatives and bio-based co-products. Within this field, future work should be completed to have a full understanding of RNG systems, including further assessment of unique plant designs, economic assumptions, and environmental boundary scopes for various feedstocks.
